picks.headline: Chourio's power meets Houser's HR problem — the ladder pick for tonight.
picks.the_thesis:
Jackson Chourio is one of the most exciting young hitters in baseball, and tonight he faces Adrian Houser — a pitcher with a 1.44 HR/9 this year. That's a significant HR rate, and Chourio has the power to take advantage. The over 1.5 total bases line is set at -105 at BetMGM, which implies roughly 51% probability. I have Chourio at 57% to hit 2+ TBs given the matchup.

Houser's 5.59 ERA and 1.56 WHIP suggest he's hittable. His HR/9 of 1.44 means he's giving up long balls at an elevated rate, and Chourio has the bat speed and power to capitalize. American Family Field has a 1.06 HR park factor, which is slightly above average. The roof will likely be open given the 83°F temperature and 0% precipitation chance.

This is the ladder pick because it's priced near even money (-105) and has a solid floor. Chourio just needs one XBH or two singles to cash. Against a pitcher who gives up hard contact, that's a reasonable expectation.
picks.the_market: Line is set at 1.5 across all books. BetMGM has the best price at -105. No significant movement.
picks.weather_park: American Family Field is a dome, but the roof will likely be open. 83°F, 0% precip, 15 mph wind outside. Neutral conditions inside.
picks.case_against: If Houser has a good day (he's capable of it), Chourio might not get the pitches to drive. The Brewers could also pull Houser early if he struggles, limiting Chourio's ABs against him.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Chourio getting 3-4 ABs against Houser, at least one XBH opportunity, and the Brewers' offense putting up runs to extend the game.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Houser gives up bombs. Chourio hits bombs. The ladder rides.
picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board at even money. Chourio just needs one XBH or two singles against a pitcher with a 1.44 HR/9. The matchup is clean, the price is right, and the variance is low.

picks.slate_analysis:
Tonight's board features seven MLB games plus Game 2 of the Stanley Cup Final between Carolina and Vegas. The MLB slate is headlined by Chris Sale at home against a Toronto team that just acquired Simeon Woods Richardson from Minnesota — a pitcher with a 7.74 ERA in 12 appearances this year. That matchup screams under, and Sale's K prop is in play. The Cubs-A's game at Wrigley drew my attention because Imanaga's HR/9 (1.67) and J.T. Ginn's walk rate (10.8 BB%) create chaos potential, but the weather report shows 13 mph wind blowing in from left-center, which suppresses fly balls and tilts the game toward the under despite the high posted total of 10.5.

I looked hard at the Brewers-Giants game, but the data confidence is only 0.4 and Coleman Crow has just 14.1 IP on the season — too thin a sample to trust. The Pirates-Astros game is a coin flip with Jared Jones's 10.38 ERA in 4.1 IP making it nearly impossible to model. The Dodgers-Diamondbacks game has Wrobleski's low K rate (16.0%) limiting prop value, and Nelson's 2.07 HR/9 makes the over tempting but the market already reflects that at 9.0.

The Stanley Cup Final is the most interesting non-MLB play. Game 1 was a 5-4 shootout, but Carolina went 12-1 in the first three rounds and their top line (Aho-Svechnikov-Jarvis) was held pointless in Game 1 — regression is coming. Vegas stole home ice, but the Hurricanes won't drop two straight at Lenovo Center. The ML at +140 offers value against a team that just played a high-event game two days ago.

The final six picks: ATL-TOR Under 7.5 (Sale dominance + weak Toronto SP), Chris Sale Ks Over 7.5 (30.2 K%, Toronto's lineup is strikeout-prone), Cubs-A's Under 10.5 (wind in at Wrigley, both SPs have control issues that extend ABs but suppress power), Hurricanes ML (bounce-back spot, home ice, elite playoff run), Jackson Chourio Total Bases Over 1.5 (Houser's 1.44 HR/9 + Chourio's power profile), and Shea Langeliers Total Bases Over 1.5 (Imanaga's 1.67 HR/9 + Langeliers' power). The ladder pick is Carolina ML — best floor on the board given their playoff dominance and home-ice advantage.

picks.headline: Svechnikov was held quiet in Game 1 — regression is coming for Carolina's top-line winger.
picks.the_thesis:
Andrei Svechnikov is one of Carolina's most dangerous offensive players, but he was held without a point in Game 1. That's an outlier — Svechnikov has 7 points (3 goals, 4 assists) in the playoffs and is a consistent shot generator. The over 2.5 SOG line is set at his median outcome, but the situational factors suggest he'll be more aggressive in Game 2.

Carolina's top line needs to respond after a quiet Game 1. Coach Rod Brind'Amour said after the loss that the team's best players need to get on the scoresheet. Svechnikov will be looking to generate more offense, which means more shots. Vegas's defense was solid in Game 1, but they allowed 27 shots — Carolina can generate chances.

The line is -118 at FanDuel, implying roughly 54% probability. I have Svechnikov at 58% to hit 3+ SOG given the bounce-back narrative and his shot volume throughout the playoffs.
picks.the_market: Line is set at 2.5 across all books. FanDuel has the best price at -118. No significant movement.
picks.weather_park: Indoor arena — no weather impact.
picks.case_against: If Vegas's defense tightens up and limits Carolina's chances, Svechnikov might not get the volume he needs. Carter Hart could also be sharper in Game 2.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Svechnikov being more aggressive, Carolina's top line generating more offense, and the Hurricanes pushing the pace at home.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Zero points in Game 1. Svechnikov doesn't do that twice.

picks.headline: Wind blowing in at Wrigley suppresses a total that's priced for a slugfest.
picks.the_thesis:
The market has this game at 10.5, which is one of the highest totals on the board. The reasoning is clear: Imanaga has a 1.67 HR/9 this year (worst in MLB), and J.T. Ginn walks too many batters (10.8 BB%). Both pitchers have blow-up potential. But the weather tells a different story.

Wrigley Field tonight will see 13 mph wind blowing in from left-center. That's significant — Wrigley is one of the windiest parks in baseball, and when the wind blows in, fly balls die. Imanaga's HR problem becomes less of an issue when the wind is knocking down fly balls. Ginn's walk rate is a concern, but walks don't score runs by themselves — you need hits to drive them in, and the Cubs' defense is elite (team Gold Glove winners).

The 10.5 is priced for a perfect storm of HR-friendly conditions. The weather says otherwise. I have this game at 8.5-9 runs, which gives us significant edge on the under.

the_data.label: Imanaga HR/9 (2026)
the_data.context: Worst in MLB, but wind helps

the_data.label: Ginn BB% (2026)
the_data.context: High, but walks don't score alone

picks.the_market: Total opened at 10.0-10.5 depending on book. FanDuel has 10.5 at -118 under, which is the best price. The market is pricing the pitchers' weaknesses but not the weather.
picks.weather_park: 82°F, 0% precip, 13 mph wind blowing in from left-center. This is a significant suppressor of fly ball distance at Wrigley.
picks.case_against: If the wind shifts (Wrigley is notorious for this), the game could turn into a slugfest. Both pitchers have blow-up potential, and one bad inning from either could push this over.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Wind staying in, both starters getting through 5 innings without a disaster, and the Cubs' defense turning batted balls into outs.
picks.scott_bot_quip: The market sees two HR-prone arms. The wind sees dinner.

The ladder pick is Carolina ML — best floor on the board given their playoff dominance and home-ice advantage.

autopsy.post_mortem: The wind data was wrong in the most damaging way possible: pre-game forecasts cited 13 mph blowing IN from left-center, but multiple pre-game sources noted the wind was actually blowing OUT — PrizePicks flagged 'wind blowing straight out at around 11 mph' giving a 34% HR boost per Ballpark Pal, and Bleed Cubbie Blue noted pregame 'the wind will be howling out to left.' The entire thesis rested on wind suppressing Imanaga's fly-ball/HR problem; instead, the park played as a hitter's environment. The result was four home runs on the night — back-to-back shots from Soderstrom and Heim off Imanaga in the 7th to make it 6-1, plus Langeliers' solo HR in the 4th and a Crow-Armstrong HR off Ginn — and the Cubs' 9th-inning walk-off rally (Happ RBI double, Ballesteros infield single, Swanson game-tying single, PCA walk-off RBI single) pushed the final to 7-6, one run over the 10.5 total.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Before posting any Wrigley under that leans on wind-in suppression, cross-check at least two real-time wind sources (e.g., Ballpark Pal + Weather.com game-time reading) within 2 hours of first pitch, and void the pick if any source shows wind blowing out — N=1, insufficient to establish rule, but the directional error here was the entire load-bearing assumption.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One wind-data miss does not confirm a systematic sourcing failure, but the magnitude of the directional error (in vs. out) warrants an immediate pre-game wind verification step in the checklist.

picks.headline: Chris Sale at home against a replacement-level starter — this total is too high.
picks.the_thesis:
Chris Sale is pitching like the 2024 Cy Young winner again. His 2.01 ERA through 11 starts is backed by elite underlying numbers — a 30.2 K% and 0.94 WHIP that suggest the surface stats aren't a mirage. He's allowed more than two earned runs just once all season, and he's 8-5 with a 2.71 career ERA against Toronto in 21 appearances.

The Blue Jays are countering with Simeon Woods Richardson, who was just acquired from Minnesota in a cash deal. Woods Richardson has a 7.74 ERA in 12 appearances (10 starts) this year — that's not a small sample fluke, that's a pitcher who can't get outs at the major league level. Toronto's offense ranks in the bottom third of the league in runs per game (4.03), and they're facing the best pitcher they'll see all week.

The market has this at 7.5, which implies roughly 4 runs for Atlanta and 3.5 for Toronto. I have Sale holding Toronto to 2-3 runs and the Braves' offense being suppressed enough by the game state (big lead early) to keep this under. The 7.5 is the right number for a neutral matchup — this is not a neutral matchup.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and hasn't moved. The market is pricing this as a standard Sale start, but the opposing pitcher is significantly worse than the average arm Sale faces. Sharp money should be on the under here.
picks.weather_park: Truist Park is a neutral park (1.02 PF runs). Weather is 76°F with 3 mph wind — no impact.
picks.case_against: If Sale has an off night (he's due for one eventually) or if the Braves' bullpen has to work extended innings, this could creep over. Toronto's lineup has some pop when they get into a bullpen early.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Sale going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, Toronto's offense staying quiet against elite stuff, and the Braves not running up the score late.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Sale on the mound, a 7.74 ERA arm on the other side. The math does itself.
picks.slate_assessment: Seven-game MLB slate plus Stanley Cup Final Game 2 tonight. Chris Sale dominates the board at home vs. Toronto, the Hurricanes need a bounce-back in a high-scoring series, and Wrigley's wind blowing in creates a pitcher-friendly environment for the Cubs-A's total.

The ladder pick is Carolina ML — best floor on the board given their playoff dominance and home-ice advantage.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire under thesis rested on Simeon Woods Richardson (7.74 ERA) starting for Toronto — but the Blue Jays actually sent Mason Fluharty (3-0, 3.97 ERA, 1.37 WHIP), a legitimate MLB arm, to the mound. The pick was made before Toronto officially announced their starter, and the 'replacement-level arm' data point that justified fading Toronto's run contribution never materialized. With Fluharty holding and Toronto winning 7-2, the over hit by nearly three runs — not because Sale imploded, but because the opposing pitcher mismatch the model priced in simply didn't exist on game day.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Do not finalize a total bet when the opposing starter is unannounced or listed as TBD; require official lineup confirmation before locking the pick, especially when the starter identity is the primary driver of the thesis — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the failure mode (betting a total against an unconfirmed starter) is structurally repeatable and worth flagging as a process check.

picks.headline: The Knicks are on an 11-game winning streak and have a significant rest advantage — they won't go quietly in Game 1.
picks.the_thesis:
The New York Knicks enter the Finals on an 11-game winning streak, including sweeps of the 76ers and Cavaliers. They've been idle since May 25, giving them a significant rest advantage over the Spurs, who just finished a grueling seven-game series against the Thunder on May 30. Jalen Brunson is averaging 26.9 PPG in the playoffs, and the Knicks shot 40% from three during their run — the best mark in the NBA.

The Spurs are rightfully favored at home, but 4.5 points is a lot to lay against a team that beat San Antonio in the NBA Cup final (124-113) and has won 8 of the last 12 meetings. The Knicks' defensive structure — built around protecting the three-point line — should force the Spurs into contested mid-range shots, which is where Wembanyama thrives but where role players struggle.

Mitchell Robinson is expected to play after hand surgery, which helps New York's rebounding and rim protection. The line is set at +4.5 at -106 on FanDuel. I have the Knicks at 57% to cover, giving us a 5+ cent edge.
picks.the_market: Line opened at +4.5 and has held steady. FanDuel at -106 is the best price. No significant movement, suggesting balanced action.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Frost Bank Center. No weather impact.
picks.case_against: The Spurs are at home in the Finals for the first time since 2014, and the crowd will be electric. Wembanyama is a matchup nightmare, and the Knicks' interior defense may struggle. De'Aaron Fox is managing an ankle sprain but is expected to play his normal minutes.
picks.what_were_betting_on: The Knicks' rest advantage translating to sharper execution, Brunson and Towns combining for 45+ points, and the Knicks' three-point shooting keeping them within striking distance.
picks.scott_bot_quip: The Knicks haven't lost in a month. The boys aren't betting against that kind of momentum.
picks.slate_assessment: MLB-heavy Wednesday with 13 games plus NBA Finals Game 1 — Cristopher Sánchez chasing history at home, Chase Burns back from illness, and Wembanyama vs. Brunson tips off in San Antonio.
picks.executive_summary: The board today is anchored by two elite pitching matchups and the NBA Finals opener. Cristopher Sánchez brings a 44⅔ scoreless-inning streak into Citizens Bank Park against Walker Buehler and the Padres — the under at 8.5 is the sharpest play on the slate. Chase Burns returns from illness to face a weak Royals lineup at Great American Ball Park, and his K prop deserves a hard look. In San Antonio, the Spurs host the Knicks in Game 1 with Victor Wembanyama healthy and the total sitting at 217.5 after the Spurs averaged 226 combined points per game in their three WCF home games. The ladder rides with the Phillies Under — Sánchez is pitching at a historic level, and the market hasn't caught up.
picks.slate_analysis:
Today's board features 13 MLB games and one NBA Finals matchup. The MLB slate is spread across all three time zones with first pitches from 3:40 PM ET through 9:40 PM PT. The NBA Finals Game 1 tips at 8:30 PM ET in San Antonio. Pricing is relatively tight across the board — most MLB moneylines sit between -150 and +150, with only the Phillies (-200) and Dodgers (-142) as clear favorites.

The top candidates that rose during my review: (1) Phillies Under 8.5 — Sánchez is on a historic scoreless streak and faces a struggling Buehler; (2) Reds ML vs. Royals — Chase Burns (1.96 ERA, 28.9 K%) returns from illness against a bottom-tier KC lineup; (3) Spurs-Knicks Over 217.5 — the Spurs averaged 226 combined points in their three WCF home games, and both teams have the offensive firepower to push this over; (4) Dodgers-Diamondbacks Under 9.5 — Ohtani on the mound with a 0.82 ERA, but the total has already moved from 8.8 to 9.5; (5) Chase Burns Ks Over 5.5 — his 28.9 K% against a KC team that strikes out at a high clip; (6) Victor Wembanyama Points Over 26.5 — he averaged 27.3 PPG in the WCF and faces a Knicks defense that will struggle to contain him.

What got cut: The Mets-Mariners game has data confidence at 0.45 and lineups aren't confirmed — too thin. The Yankees-Guardians game has Gerrit Cole on a pitch count (only 12.2 IP this season) and the total moved from 7.5 to 8.5 — no edge. The Dodgers-Diamondbacks Under looked appealing with Ohtani pitching, but the total already moved a full run and Gallen's 5.16 ERA creates variance. The Braves-Blue Jays game has two mediocre starters (Holmes 3.95 ERA, Corbin 3.65 ERA) and data confidence at 0.4 — pass. The Pirates-Astros game has only one book reporting and data confidence at 0.3 — too thin.

The final six picks: Phillies Under 8.5 (Sánchez historic streak, Buehler struggling), Reds ML (Burns elite, Royals weak), Spurs-Knicks Over 217.5 (WCF home scoring trends), Chase Burns Ks Over 5.5 (elite K rate vs. high-K offense), Victor Wembanyama Points Over 26.5 (WCF form, Knicks matchup), and Knicks +4.5 (11-game win streak, rest advantage). The ladder pick is the Phillies Under — Sánchez is the best pitcher in baseball right now, and the market hasn't fully adjusted to his dominance.

picks.headline: Wembanyama averaged 27.3 PPG in the WCF — the Knicks don't have anyone who can guard him.
picks.the_thesis:
Victor Wembanyama is the reigning Defensive Player of the Year and the Western Conference Finals MVP. He averaged 27.3 PPG, 10.9 RPG, and 3.1 APG across the seven WCF games against the Thunder, and he's healthy heading into Game 1. The Knicks' defensive structure is built around protecting the three-point line, which leaves his post-up and mid-range game available early.

Karl-Anthony Towns and Mitchell Robinson will take turns guarding Wembanyama, but neither has the length or mobility to truly contest his shots. The Knicks' strategy will likely involve physical play and double-teams, but Wembanyama has shown he can score through contact and find open teammates when doubled.

The line is set at 26.5 points at -118 on FanDuel. His WCF average of 27.3 PPG suggests this is a coin flip at worst. I have him at 61% to hit 27+ points, giving us a 5+ cent edge.
picks.the_market: Line is set at 26.5 points at -118 on FanDuel, -105 at BetMGM. FanDuel has the better price for the over. No significant movement since open.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Frost Bank Center. No weather impact.
picks.case_against: The Knicks could throw multiple bodies at Wembanyama and force his teammates to beat them. If the Spurs' role players get hot, Wembanyama's usage could dip. Game 1 rust is also a factor.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Wembanyama getting 20+ FGA, the Knicks' interior defense struggling to contain him, and the Spurs running their offense through their franchise player in a Finals home game.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Wemby is 7'4" and the Knicks are bringing a step ladder. Over.
picks.slate_assessment: MLB-heavy Wednesday with 13 games plus NBA Finals Game 1 — Cristopher Sánchez chasing history at home, Chase Burns back from illness, and Wembanyama vs. Brunson tips off in San Antonio.

autopsy.post_mortem: Wembanyama finished with exactly 26 points — one short of the 26.5 line — but the story isn't bad luck at the margin. He shot 6-of-21 from the field (2-of-9 from three) with six turnovers, and the Knicks successfully kept him away from the rim: only 5 of his 21 attempts came at the basket, forcing him into the perimeter jumpers the thesis assumed would be available in the paint. The model leaned on his WCF PPG average (27.3) while underweighting his full 17-game playoff PPG (23.2) and ignored the specific defensive wrinkle — the Knicks didn't leave the paint open as predicted; they funneled him to the perimeter and let him shoot over traffic. He self-described the performance as 'bad,' and the 12-of-13 free throw line production (the only thing that got him to 26) masked a genuinely poor shooting night.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a player's series-specific sample (WCF, N=7) diverges materially from their full playoff sample (N=17), weight the larger sample more heavily in the points projection — especially when the opponent's defensive scheme is structurally different from the prior series opponent; validate this rule at N≥10 before applying as a hard filter.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a single-game observation and could reflect Finals debut variance as much as a systematic modeling error.

picks.headline: Burns has a 28.9 K% and averages 10+ Ks per 9 IP — the Royals strike out at a high clip.
picks.the_thesis:
Chase Burns is a strikeout machine. His 28.9 K% ranks among the league's elite, and he's averaging 10.1 K/9 this season. His fastball averages 98.1 mph with elite induced vertical break, and his slider generates a 56% whiff rate. In his last three starts, he's averaged 7+ Ks per outing.

The Royals are a high-strikeout team — their offense ranks in the bottom third of MLB in contact rate. Stephen Kolek's soft-tossing approach won't help them adjust to Burns' velocity. The line is set at 5.5 Ks at -107 on DraftKings, which implies ~51.7% probability. I have Burns at 63% to hit 6+ Ks, giving us an 11+ cent edge.

Burns was scratched Monday due to illness, but he's confirmed to pitch Wednesday. If he's at full strength, 6+ Ks is a near-certainty against this lineup.
picks.the_market: Line is set at 5.5 Ks at -107 on DraftKings. No movement since open. The market is undervaluing Burns' strikeout upside against a weak-contact team.
picks.weather_park: Great American Ball Park, 79°F, wind in from left field. No impact on strikeouts.
picks.case_against: Burns was scratched Monday due to illness — if he's not 100%, his velocity could dip and his K rate could suffer. The Royals could also shorten at-bats and put balls in play early in counts.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Burns going 6+ IP with 7+ Ks, the Royals swinging and missing at his slider, and Burns' fastball velocity staying at 98+ mph.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Burns throws gas and the Royals swing at air. Math checks out.

picks.headline: The Spurs averaged 226 combined points per game in their three WCF home games — Game 1 of the Finals should follow suit.
picks.the_thesis:
The San Antonio Spurs just finished a seven-game Western Conference Finals against the Thunder, and their three home games averaged 226.4 combined points — all three went over the total. Victor Wembanyama averaged 27.3 PPG, 10.9 RPG, and 3.1 APG in the WCF, and he's healthy heading into Game 1. The Knicks, meanwhile, are on an 11-game winning streak and have been idle since May 25, giving them a significant rest advantage.

The Knicks shot 40% from three during their playoff run — the best mark in the NBA — while holding opponents to 30.5%. Jalen Brunson is averaging 26.9 PPG in the playoffs, and Karl-Anthony Towns provides an inside-outside presence that should generate efficient offense. Mitchell Robinson is expected to play after hand surgery, which helps New York's rebounding and rim protection.

The total opened at 217.5 and hasn't moved. The Spurs' WCF home games suggest this number is too low. Both teams have the offensive firepower to push this over, and Game 1 of the Finals typically features some rust and adjustment — but also high-stakes intensity that drives scoring.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 217.5 and has held steady across both books. FanDuel at -108 is the best price. No significant movement, suggesting balanced action.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Frost Bank Center. No weather impact.
picks.case_against: The Knicks' rest advantage could lead to a slower pace as they work off rust. De'Aaron Fox is managing an ankle sprain and may be less aggressive. Game 1 of the Finals can be tight and defensive as teams feel each other out.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Both teams scoring 108+ points, Wembanyama and Brunson combining for 50+ points, and the pace staying above 95 possessions.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Wemby and Brunson in the Finals. The boys are watching history — might as well bet on it.

autopsy.post_mortem: Final was 105-95 (200 combined), missing the Over 217.5 by 17.5. Both primary scorers were ice-cold — Wembanyama shot 3-of-13 and Brunson shot 7-of-22 through three quarters — and the Spurs bench outside of Dylan Harper combined for just 4 points. The thesis extrapolated Spurs WCF home-game pace (226.4 PPG) directly onto a Finals Game 1, ignoring that opponent quality, defensive intensity, and game-script all reset in a new series. The 'case against' even flagged a potential defensive feel-out game, but the model still leaned into the over.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Do not use a team's same-round home-game scoring averages as a baseline for a new series — opponent defensive quality resets; require at least one data point from the new matchup or a head-to-head regular season pace proxy before projecting totals above the market line. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one game where both stars shot poorly and a bench went cold is noise, not a pattern.

picks.headline: Cristopher Sánchez is chasing Orel Hershiser's record with 44⅔ scoreless innings — the best pitcher in baseball faces a struggling Walker Buehler.
picks.the_thesis:
Cristopher Sánchez enters Wednesday's start riding a scoreless streak of 44⅔ innings — the seventh-longest in MLB history and a new Phillies franchise record, surpassing Grover Cleveland Alexander's 115-year-old mark. He's the first pitcher since Orel Hershiser in 1988 to go an entire calendar month without allowing a run (minimum 30 IP). His 1.47 ERA leads all qualified starters, and his 29.4 K% is elite. With a scoreless first inning, he'll pass Carl Hubbell for the longest scoreless streak ever by a left-handed pitcher.

Walker Buehler, meanwhile, has been mediocre at best — a 4.88 ERA with a 1.32 WHIP and only 17 IP per start over his last three outings. The Padres offense has been league-average at 3.88 R/G, but they're facing a pitcher who hasn't allowed a run in over a month. Citizens Bank Park is HR-friendly (1.10 PF), but Sánchez's 0.34 HR/9 suggests he's not giving up the long ball regardless of venue.

The total opened at 7.5 and has moved to 8.5, but that movement is driven by the park factor and Buehler's struggles — not by any expectation that Sánchez will suddenly become hittable. The market is pricing in regression that hasn't materialized. This is the highest-floor play on the board.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has moved to 8.5 across the board. Only FanDuel is reporting, so book count is thin, but the movement suggests public money on the over. The under at -124 still offers value given Sánchez's dominance.
picks.weather_park: Citizens Bank Park is HR-friendly (1.10 PF), but Sánchez's 0.34 HR/9 neutralizes the park effect. Weather is a non-factor for this indoor-adjacent venue.
picks.case_against: Buehler's struggles could lead to a short outing and early bullpen exposure. If the Phillies pen gives up runs in the 5th-7th innings, the over comes into play. The total has already moved a full run, suggesting sharp money may have already taken the under.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Sánchez continuing his historic run with 6+ scoreless innings, Buehler lasting 5 IP with 2-3 ER, and both bullpens holding serve.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Sánchez is pitching like he's got a grudge against the concept of earned runs. The boys ride with history.
picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board — Sánchez is the best pitcher in baseball right now, and the market hasn't fully adjusted. Even if Buehler implodes, Sánchez's side of the ledger stays clean.

picks.headline: Jack Eichel has been a shot machine in the playoffs, and Carolina allows volume.
picks.the_thesis:
Jack Eichel has been one of the best players in the NHL playoffs. He's averaging 3.5 SOG/G in the postseason, and his usage on the power play ensures he'll get quality looks. Eichel is the engine of the Vegas offense, and he's been shooting at will.

Carolina's defense is elite, but they allow shots — they've faced 30+ shots in multiple playoff games. Frederik Andersen has been excellent, but volume is volume. Eichel will get his chances, and he needs just 3 shots on goal to cash this prop.

The market has this at -104 at FanDuel, which implies 51% probability. My fair line is 56%, giving us a 5-cent edge. Eichel's floor is high, and the matchup is favorable for shot volume.
picks.the_market: Line is -104 at FanDuel, -110 at DraftKings. The market is pricing this as a coin flip, but Eichel's volume and usage give us an edge.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Lenovo Center. No weather impact.
picks.case_against: Carolina's defense is elite and could limit Eichel's quality looks. Andersen has been excellent, and the Canes could control possession. Eichel could have a quiet night if Vegas falls behind early.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Eichel getting 15+ minutes of ice time, shooting at his playoff rate, and Carolina allowing enough volume for him to hit 3 SOG.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Eichel shoots. A lot. Carolina can't stop that.
picks.slate_assessment: Sixteen-game MLB board plus Game 1 of the Stanley Cup Final. Two elite SP matchups (Harrison vs SF, Martin vs MIN) anchor the slate, with several totals and props offering clear edges.
picks.executive_summary: The board tonight is headlined by two breakout aces: Kyle Harrison (1.57 ERA) hosts San Francisco in Milwaukee, and Davis Martin (2.00 ERA) faces Minnesota at Target Field. Both games feature dominant pitching against middling offenses in neutral-to-pitcher-friendly parks, making the unders the sharpest plays on the slate. The Stanley Cup Final opener in Carolina adds a low-scoring hockey angle. The ladder rides with the Brewers-Giants Under 7.5 — Harrison's floor is elite, the Giants offense is anemic, and the market hasn't moved off the number.
picks.slate_analysis:
Tonight's board features 16 MLB games and Game 1 of the Stanley Cup Final between Carolina and Vegas. The pricing climate is soft — most totals sit near consensus, and only a handful of moneylines offer juice better than -130. The data confidence flags are low across the board (0.4-0.45 on most games), which limits conviction on sides, but the pitching matchups in Milwaukee and Minnesota stand out.

The top candidates that rose during my review: (1) Brewers-Giants Under 7.5 — Kyle Harrison's 1.57 ERA and 29.6% K rate against a Giants offense averaging 3.9 R/G is the cleanest pitching edge on the board. (2) White Sox ML at Minnesota — Davis Martin's 2.00 ERA and breakout season against a Twins lineup that just lost to him last week. (3) Carolina Hurricanes ML in Game 1 — the Canes are 12-1 in the playoffs, have home ice, and face a Vegas team that's been grinding through tougher matchups. (4) Yankees-Guardians Under 7.5 — Cam Schlittler's 1.50 ERA and 0.85 WHIP against a Cleveland offense that struggles vs RHP. (5) Braves-Blue Jays Under 7.5 — Bryce Elder (2.50 ERA) vs Kevin Gausman (3.13 ERA) in a neutral park. (6) Bobby Witt Jr. Total Bases Over 1.5 — Witt is the best hitter in the Royals lineup facing Andrew Abbott, who has a 1.30 HR/9 and 10% BB rate.

What got cut: The Yankees-Guardians Under looked sharp, but the data confidence is 0.4 and lineups aren't confirmed — I can't trust it enough to include. The Braves-Blue Jays Under has two solid SPs, but the data confidence is also 0.4 and the market is already priced efficiently at 7.5. The Phillies-Padres game has Aaron Nola struggling (5.72 ERA) and Randy Vásquez pitching well, but the park is HR-boosting and the data confidence is low. The Cardinals-Rangers game is a true pick'em with no edge. The Diamondbacks-Dodgers game has Eric Lauer regressing (6.69 L3 ERA) but the total is already at 9.5 and the market knows it.

The final six picks: (1) Brewers-Giants Under 7.5 — Harrison's dominance, SF's weak offense, indoor park. (2) White Sox ML at Minnesota — Martin's breakout, Prielipp's struggles, value at -120. (3) Carolina Hurricanes ML — 12-1 playoff run, home ice, elite defense. (4) Bobby Witt Jr. Total Bases Over 1.5 — best hitter vs mediocre SP, HR-boosting park. (5) Ketel Marte Total Bases Over 1.5 — Lauer's regression, Chase Field HR factor, Marte's elite bat. (6) Jack Eichel SOG Over 2.5 — Eichel averaging 3.5 SOG/G in playoffs, Carolina allows shots, even money price. The ladder pick is the Brewers-Giants Under 7.5 — Harrison's floor is the highest on the board, the Giants can't score, and the market hasn't moved.

picks.headline: Ketel Marte faces a regressing Eric Lauer in a HR-friendly dome.
picks.the_thesis:
Eric Lauer has been one of the worst pitchers in baseball over his last three starts. His 6.69 L3 ERA is backed by a 2.57 HR/9 on the season — that's a home run every 3.5 innings. Lauer's stuff has regressed, and the Dodgers are using him as a fill-in starter. Ketel Marte is one of the best hitters in the NL, and he feasts on struggling pitchers.

Chase Field is a neutral-to-hitter-friendly park (1.07 HR PF), and the dome eliminates weather variance. Marte bats in the heart of the Diamondbacks lineup and will see 4-5 plate appearances. He needs just 2 total bases — a double, two singles, or a HR — to cash this prop.

The market has this at -105 at BetMGM, which implies 51.2% probability. My fair line is 57%, giving us a 6-cent edge. Marte's floor is high, and Lauer is hittable.
picks.the_market: Line is -105 at BetMGM, -106 at DraftKings. The market is pricing this as a coin flip, but Lauer's regression and Marte's quality give us an edge.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Chase Field. Park is neutral-to-hitter-friendly (1.07 HR PF). No weather impact.
picks.case_against: Lauer could have a bounce-back start — his stuff isn't terrible, just inconsistent. Marte could go 0-for-4 on a bad night. The Dodgers offense is elite and could put Arizona in a hole early, limiting Marte's opportunities.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Marte getting 4-5 PA, making hard contact against a HR-prone lefty, and the park doing the rest.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Lauer's been a piñata. Marte's got the bat.

The model correctly identified Lauer as hittable but failed to account for which side of the ball Marte plays on — the Dodgers' lineup faced Lauer, not the Diamondbacks'.

picks.pick: Bobby Witt Jr. Total Bases Over 1.5
picks.headline: The best hitter in the Royals lineup faces a struggling lefty in a HR-boosting park.
picks.the_thesis:
Bobby Witt Jr. is one of the best hitters in baseball. He's been the engine of the Royals offense all season, and his power-speed combination makes him a threat every time he steps to the plate. Against lefties, Witt has historically crushed — and Andrew Abbott is a lefty with a 1.30 HR/9 and 10% BB rate. Abbott's command issues mean more pitches in the zone, and Witt doesn't miss mistakes.

Great American Ball Park is one of the most HR-friendly parks in baseball (1.18 HR PF). The weather is mild (75°F, wind L-to-R), which won't suppress fly balls. Witt bats in the heart of the Royals lineup and will see 4-5 plate appearances. He needs just 2 total bases — a double, two singles, or a HR — to cash this prop.

The market has this at -120 at BetMGM, which implies 54.5% probability. My fair line is 58%, giving us a 3.5-cent edge. Witt's floor is high, and the matchup is favorable.
picks.the_market: Line is -120 at BetMGM, +113 at DraftKings. The discrepancy suggests some books are sharper on Witt's value. We're taking the best price at MGM.
picks.weather_park: Great American Ball Park, 75°F, wind L-to-R. Park is HR-boosting (1.18 PF). No weather suppression.
picks.case_against: Abbott has been better than his ERA suggests, and his 15.5% K rate means he's not giving up free passes. Witt could go 0-for-4 on a bad night. Props are high-variance by nature.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Witt getting 4-5 PA, making hard contact against a HR-prone lefty, and the park doing the rest.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Witt vs. a lefty in Cincinnati. Do the math.

The pick leaned on Abbott's season-aggregate HR/9 (1.30) and BB% (10%) without adequately discounting his recent hot stretch — a classic recency-blind model error.

picks.headline: The Hurricanes are 12-1 in the playoffs and host Game 1 of the Stanley Cup Final.
picks.the_thesis:
Carolina has been the best team in the NHL playoffs by a wide margin. Their 12-1 record includes back-to-back sweeps of Ottawa and Philadelphia, plus a five-game dismissal of Montreal in the Eastern Conference Final. They've allowed just 21 goals in 13 games — that's 1.62 GA/G, which is historically elite. Frederik Andersen has been a Conn Smythe frontrunner, and the defense in front of him has been suffocating.

Vegas is a worthy opponent — they swept Colorado in the Western Conference Final and have Mitch Marner leading all playoff scorers with 21 points. Carter Hart has been excellent in goal. But the Golden Knights are on the road, facing a team that hasn't lost at home in the playoffs, in a building that will be rocking for the franchise's first Cup Final since 2006.

The market has Carolina at -150, which implies 60% win probability. My fair line is 62%, giving us a small but real edge. Home ice matters in the Cup Final, and Carolina's defensive structure is built for low-scoring games. This is a grind-it-out series, and the Canes have the edge in Game 1.
picks.the_market: Carolina opened as -150 favorites and the line has held. The series price is -150 Carolina, +125 Vegas. No significant movement suggests the market is comfortable with Carolina as favorites.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Lenovo Center. No weather impact.
picks.case_against: Vegas has playoff experience — they won the Cup in 2023 and know how to handle the pressure. Carter Hart has been excellent, and Marner is playing the best hockey of his career. Carolina hasn't been tested in a close series yet, and Game 1 of the Cup Final is a different animal.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Carolina's elite defense continuing to suffocate opponents, Andersen staying sharp, and the home crowd providing an edge in a tight game.
picks.scott_bot_quip: The Canes are 12-1 and haven't lost at home. Vegas is walking into a buzzsaw.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis held for exactly 12 minutes. Carolina jumped out 2-0 on Ehlers goals at 0:25 and 12:08 of the first — the pick was alive and the defensive structure looked intact. Then Vegas did something road teams had never done in Cup Final Game 1 history: they erased a multi-goal deficit (0-55 all-time entering tonight) and won 5-4. The game was tied 4-4 with 8:41 left in the third after Gostisbehere's tying goal, meaning Carolina had a legitimate chance to win regulation. The killing blow was Hertl's slot goal at 16:36 of the third off a Sissons feed — a single shot in a game that was dead-even for 55+ minutes. Andersen allowed 5 goals on just 23 shots, a .783 SV% that was a complete outlier against his .944 playoff mark; Carolina's top line of Aho/Jarvis/Svechnikov went pointless and was outscored 1-0 at 5v5. This was not a bad pick — it was a correctly reasoned pick that ran into a historically improbable Vegas comeback, a cold night from the Conn Smythe frontrunner, and one late goal that could have gone either way.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Carolina's defensive structure and Andersen's save percentage remain elite indicators — one outlier game does not invalidate the thesis.

picks.headline: Davis Martin's breakout season continues against a Twins team he just dominated.
picks.the_thesis:
Davis Martin has been one of the best stories in baseball this season. His 2.00 ERA through 11 starts is backed by elite underlying metrics: a 2.31 FIP (4th in MLB), a 27% K rate (80th percentile), and a 5.3% BB rate (92nd percentile). He's not just getting lucky — he's pitching like an ace. Last week, Martin held the Twins to two hits over six innings in a 6-2 win at home.

The Twins counter with Connor Prielipp, a lefty with a 5.13 ERA and 10.3% BB rate. Prielipp has struggled with command all season, and his 33.1 IP sample is too small to trust. The White Sox offense (4.68 R/G) has been solid, and they've won 12 of their last 16 games. Minnesota is 4.66 R/G but has been inconsistent against quality pitching.

Target Field is a neutral park (0.98 runs PF), and the weather is mild (no wind impact). The market has the White Sox as slight favorites (-120 at FD), which feels right given Martin's form. My fair line is -135, giving us a 5-cent edge. Martin's floor is 6 innings of 2-run ball, and the Twins don't have the lineup to overcome that.
picks.the_market: White Sox opened as slight favorites and the line has held. The -120 at FanDuel is the best price among our books. No significant movement suggests the market is comfortable with this number.
picks.weather_park: Target Field, 73°F, mild wind. Park is neutral (0.98 runs PF). No weather impact expected.
picks.case_against: Martin is due for regression — his 2.00 ERA is well below his 2.31 FIP, and the Twins lineup is better than their recent results suggest. Prielipp could settle in and give Minnesota a chance. Road favorites in baseball are always risky.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Martin continuing his dominance, the White Sox offense providing early run support, and Prielipp's command issues showing up again.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Martin's been unhittable. The Twins already know.
picks.slate_assessment: Sixteen-game MLB board plus Game 1 of the Stanley Cup Final. Two elite SP matchups (Harrison vs SF, Martin vs MIN) anchor the slate, with several totals and props offering clear edges.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis was built on Martin's season-long dominance, but two critical factors were underweighted: (1) Martin's career ERA against Minnesota entering this start was 5.29 in seven games — a specific, documented vulnerability that the pick glossed over in favor of his one dominant outing five days prior; (2) the 'short turnaround repeat matchup' risk cut both ways — Prielipp, who got shelled in that prior start, went a career-high six innings and struck out seven, while the Twins' bottom-of-the-order hitters torched Martin for a season-high 10 hits and 6 runs in 4⅔ innings, blowing up the 'floor of 6 innings of 2-run ball' assumption entirely. The White Sox took a 3-0 lead in the third on Acuña's aggressive baserunning and a Vargas two-run single, but Minnesota answered with a four-run fourth inning — Martin walked two batters in the fifth and allowed another run before being pulled. The Twins bullpen then struck out the side in the seventh with the tying run at second, slamming the door. The pick leaned on recency bias (one great start vs. MIN) while ignoring Martin's documented career struggles against this specific opponent.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Before backing a starter as a repeat-matchup favorite within 7 days, cross-check career splits against that opponent — if career ERA vs. that team is 1.5+ runs above season ERA, flag as a MODEL risk and require a line of -110 or better to proceed; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a pattern to watch, not a validated filter.

picks.headline: Kyle Harrison's elite form meets San Francisco's anemic offense in a pitcher-friendly dome.
picks.the_thesis:
Kyle Harrison has been one of the best pitchers in baseball over the past month. His 1.57 ERA through 51.2 innings is backed by a 29.6% K rate and a 0.53 HR/9 — that's elite run prevention, not luck. He's thrown three consecutive scoreless outings, including an 11-K gem against the Cubs. The Brewers are getting a true ace performance every fifth day.

The Giants counter with Trevor McDonald, a rookie with a 4.34 ERA and just 29 IP on the season. McDonald's 0.62 HR/9 looks good, but his 22.1% K rate and 4.9% BB rate suggest he's pitching to contact — and the Brewers offense (4.98 R/G) can punish that. However, this game is about Harrison's floor, not McDonald's ceiling. San Francisco is averaging just 3.9 R/G on the season, and their lineup has no answer for a lefty with Harrison's stuff.

American Family Field is a neutral park (1.0 runs PF, 1.06 HR PF), but the dome eliminates weather variance. The market has this at 7.5 across all books, and the under is getting slight juice (-115 at MGM). My fair line is 6.8 runs — that's a 10-cent edge on the under. Harrison's floor is 6 innings of 1-2 run ball, and the Giants don't have the firepower to push this over.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and hasn't moved. The under is getting slight juice (-115 at MGM, -124 at FD, -125 at DK), suggesting the market respects Harrison but hasn't fully priced in his recent dominance.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at American Family Field — no weather impact. Park is neutral for runs (1.0 PF).
picks.case_against: McDonald has been better than his ERA suggests (0.62 HR/9), and the Brewers offense can score in bunches. If Harrison has an off night or exits early, the Brewers pen has been shaky. The Giants also have nothing to lose and could play loose.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Harrison going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, the Giants offense staying cold, and the Brewers not needing to chase runs late.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Harrison's been untouchable. The Giants brought a knife to a gunfight.
picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board. Harrison's last three starts: 18 IP, 0 ER. The Giants can't score. The market hasn't moved. This is the ladder.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis was built entirely on Harrison suppressing the Giants — and he did exactly that, striking out 12 over 5.2 innings and allowing just 1 run. The model failure was on the other half of the equation: McDonald. The pick treated McDonald's 4.34 ERA as a manageable risk, but the Brewers jumped on him immediately — Jake Bauers hit a 3-run homer in the first inning after Yelich and Chourio walked, and Milwaukee built a 4-0 lead before the Giants could respond. The Giants briefly made it 4-3 in the eighth on pinch-hit RBI singles, but the Brewers then blew it open with a 4-run bottom of the eighth against Tristan Beck — Yelich, Chourio, and Turang all drove in runs — pushing the final to 8-3. The under was never really alive: Milwaukee's home run-scoring rate (5.42 R/G at home) and McDonald's contact-heavy profile were a combustible combination that the model underweighted in favor of fixating on Harrison's floor.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When the under thesis depends on a single elite starter suppressing one side, require the opposing starter's FIP to be within 1.0 of the total-implied run expectation per team — if the gap is wider, the under is structurally one-sided and the total is more likely to be driven by the weaker arm; flag as MODEL RISK before publishing. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor for pattern across similar 'ace carries the under' setups.)
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule.

picks.headline: Two HR-suppressing profiles meet at Busch Stadium with wind blowing in.
picks.the_thesis:
Both pitchers in this game have HR-suppressing profiles. McGreevy's 1.2 HR/9 is elevated, but Busch Stadium's 0.93 HR park factor should help. deGrom's 1.98 HR/9 is alarming, but he's still elite at limiting baserunners (1.01 WHIP).

picks.headline: T-Mobile Park suppresses runs, Hancock has been excellent, and the Mets are starting a reliever-turned-opener.
picks.the_thesis:
T-Mobile Park is one of the most pitcher-friendly parks in baseball. Its runs park factor of 0.93 and HR park factor of 0.93 suppress offense, and the Mariners have been built around pitching and defense for years.

Emerson Hancock has been excellent in 2026 — a 2.78 ERA with a 1.01 WHIP and a 25.1% strikeout rate. He's been consistent, going 6+ innings in most of his starts, and his underlying metrics support the surface numbers.

The Mets are starting Austin Warren, a reliever-turned-opener with limited innings. His 1.40 ERA is backed by a 1.19 WHIP, but he's only thrown 19.1 innings this season. The Mets will likely need multiple relievers to cover this game, which creates variance.

The total has moved slightly from 7.33 to 7.5, but the underlying matchup still favors the under. Both offenses are league average, and the park suppresses runs.

picks.headline: deGrom struggled in May (5.72 ERA, nine HRs allowed). McGreevy has been solid at home.
picks.the_thesis:
Jacob deGrom is still Jacob deGrom, but May was rough. He posted a 5.72 ERA with a .332 wOBA and allowed nine of his 13 home runs on the season. His HR/9 of 1.98 is alarming for a pitcher of his caliber, and Busch Stadium's HR-suppressing park factor (0.93) should help the Cardinals.

Michael McGreevy has been a pleasant surprise for St. Louis. His 2.98 ERA is backed by a 1.09 WHIP and a 6.3% walk rate. His xERA of 5.63 suggests he's been lucky, but he's been effective at home and the Cardinals have been strong at Busch Stadium this season.

The line has moved toward the Cardinals (from +106 to -104), suggesting sharp money on the home side. deGrom's May struggles and McGreevy's home splits create value on St. Louis.
picks.the_market: Line opened at Cardinals +106 and has moved to -104 — a significant swing toward the home side. The market is pricing this as a coin flip, but the underlying matchup and deGrom's May struggles suggest value on St. Louis.
picks.weather_park: Busch Stadium is HR-suppressing (PF 0.93). Wind blowing in from CF at 6 mph. Weather is clear, 82°F.
picks.case_against: deGrom is still deGrom — he can dominate any lineup on any given night. McGreevy's xERA of 5.63 suggests he's been lucky, and regression could come at any time. The Rangers offense has been solid, and deGrom's strikeout rate (29.4%) is still elite.
picks.what_were_betting_on: McGreevy going 6+ innings with 3 or fewer earned runs, the Cardinals offense tagging deGrom for a few runs, and Busch Stadium suppressing the long ball.
picks.scott_bot_quip: deGrom's May was a horror movie. The Cardinals are betting on a sequel.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis was built on deGrom's May struggles (5.72 ERA), but the pick ignored a critical counter-signal hiding in plain sight: deGrom entered this start with a 3.77 season ERA, a 10.56 K/9, and a .205 opponent batting average over his last 10 outings — he was trending sharply in the right direction, not still mired in May. On the night, he was vintage deGrom: five shutout innings, 4 hits, 1 walk, 8 strikeouts, earning his 100th career win. The Cardinals had runners on in the 2nd inning but deGrom struck out Walker and Torres to escape, then held them scoreless through 5. McGreevy, meanwhile, gave up the two decisive runs in the 4th (Nimmo scored on a Duran double) and 5th (Jansen walked, stole second, scored on a Pederson single), and the Cardinals' lone run — a Masyn Winn solo homer off reliever Peyton Gray in the 6th — was too little, too late. The bullpen held the 2-1 lead and Latz closed it in the 9th. The model leaned on a stale May ERA snapshot while deGrom's recent form and elite strikeout profile — the exact 'case against' acknowledged in the pick — was the actual signal.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a starter's trailing-month ERA diverges significantly from their season ERA AND their last-10-start ERA is trending down, weight the recent trend over the monthly split before fading them — but validate this rule across a minimum of 20+ comparable 'ERA-rebound' starts before treating it as a systematic edge.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule; this is a single-game observation and could easily be noise.

picks.headline: E-Rod's 2.31 ERA is propped up by a 4.43 xERA — regression is coming, but so is the under.
picks.the_thesis:
Eduardo Rodriguez has been one of the best stories in baseball this season — a 5-1 record with a 2.31 ERA after two disastrous seasons in Arizona. But the underlying numbers tell a different story. His xERA sits at 4.43, a full two runs higher than his surface ERA, suggesting he's been significantly overperforming his peripherals. His chase rate is 38th percentile, his strikeout rate is 18% (23rd percentile), and his whiff rate is below average. The ERA is real but fragile.

Emmet Sheehan, on the other side, tells the opposite story. His 4.70 ERA looks alarming, but his xERA of 3.86 shows he's been somewhat unlucky. He's struggled on the road (6.52 ERA), but he's facing an Arizona offense that has dropped three straight and struggles to make consistent contact outside the zone.

The total has climbed from 8.5 at open to 9.25 now, creating value on the under. Both pitchers have regression risk, but the market has overcorrected. Chase Field is neutral (runs PF 1.01), and neither offense is elite. I'm betting on both pitchers to be better than their worst-case scenarios.

picks.headline: Joe Ryan's six-start dominance streak meets a rebuilding White Sox club with a TBD arm.
picks.the_thesis:
Joe Ryan has been one of the most consistent starters in baseball over the past month. He hasn't allowed more than two earned runs in six straight starts, posting a 2.94 ERA with a 0.93 WHIP and a 27.5% strikeout rate. His command has been elite — a 5.5% walk rate is top-tier — and his underlying metrics (FIP, xERA) support the surface numbers. This is a pitcher who's pitching to his talent level, not running hot.

The White Sox, meanwhile, are in full rebuild mode. Their Opening Day rotation featured Shane Smith, Sean Burke, Anthony Kay, Davis Martin, and Erick Fedde — none of whom would crack a contender's top three. Tonight's starter is TBD, likely David Sandlin, a rookie who made his MLB debut last week and allowed one run in one hit with four strikeouts against the Twins. That's a tiny sample, and the Twins lineup has seen him once already. The White Sox offense ranks near the bottom of the league in most categories, and they're on the road against a pitcher who's been dominant.

The line has moved against the Twins — from -158 at open to -102 now — which is unusual given Ryan's form. That movement suggests sharp money on the White Sox, but I'm not buying it. The underlying matchup still favors Minnesota, and the line movement has created value on the home side.
picks.the_market: Line opened at Twins -158 and has moved all the way to -102 — a 10-cent swing toward the White Sox. That's unusual given Ryan's form, but it's created value on the home side. The market is pricing this as a coin flip, but the underlying matchup says otherwise.
picks.weather_park: Target Field is neutral (runs PF 0.98, HR PF 0.97). Weather is clear, no wind concerns.
picks.case_against: The line movement is real — sharp money has come in on the White Sox, and there may be information I don't have (injury, lineup change, etc.). If Ryan exits early or the White Sox starter outperforms expectations, this could bust. The Twins have also been inconsistent this season (15-20 record), and their bullpen has had some rough patches.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Joe Ryan going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer earned runs, the Twins offense providing enough support against a TBD arm, and the bullpen closing it out.
picks.scott_bot_quip: The sharps moved this line 10 cents. I'm betting they're wrong.
picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board — elite pitcher, weak opponent, value created by line movement. This is the play you want for a ladder challenge.

The final six picks: (1) Twins ML — best floor, best pitcher, weakest opponent. (2) Dodgers-Diamondbacks Under 9.0 — regression risk on both sides, total has climbed too high. (3) Angels ML — Freeland has been a disaster, Soriano has been solid. (4) Cardinals ML — deGrom's May struggles, McGreevy's home splits, Busch Stadium suppresses HRs. (5) Mariners Under 7.5 — T-Mobile Park is pitcher-friendly, Hancock has been excellent, Warren is a reliever-turned-opener with limited innings. (6) Rangers-Cardinals Under 7.5 — both pitchers have HR-suppressing profiles, Busch Stadium wind blowing in. The ladder pick is Twins ML — Ryan's form, the White Sox's weakness, and the line movement creating value at -102.

picks.headline: Judge faces a struggling lefty with a 2.05 HR/9 — total bases over is the play.
picks.the_thesis:
Aaron Judge is one of baseball's most dangerous hitters, and today he faces Jacob Lopez — a lefty with a 5.73 ERA, 1.77 WHIP, and 2.05 HR/9. Lopez can't keep the ball in the park, and Judge is the exact type of power hitter who will make him pay.

The total bases line at 1.5 is conservative for Judge, who regularly produces multi-base games. He needs just one XBH or two singles to cash this prop. Against a lefty who gives up hard contact, Judge should have multiple opportunities to drive the ball.

Sutter Health Park is a neutral venue (1.02 runs PF, 0.98 HR PF), and the 83°F temperature with 5 mph wind L-to-R won't suppress offense. The Yankees are heavy favorites (-184), suggesting they'll have plenty of plate appearances to work with.
picks.the_market: Line set at 1.5 with over at -130 on BetMGM (best price). DraftKings has it at -144, so there's value shopping to BetMGM.
picks.weather_park: Sutter Health Park, 83°F, wind 5 mph L-to-R. Neutral conditions, warm temperature helps offense.
picks.case_against: Judge could be pitched around if the A's don't want to give him anything to hit. Lopez might also be pulled early if he struggles, limiting Judge's ABs against him. The A's bullpen could be tougher.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Judge getting 4+ plate appearances against a struggling lefty, driving at least one ball for extra bases, and the Yankees' offense creating run-scoring opportunities.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Lopez gives up homers like he's allergic to keeping the ball in the park. Judge will feast.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis was sound — Judge faced a struggling Lopez (5.73 ERA, 2.05 HR/9) and the Yankees won the game. But Lopez and the A's bullpen pitched around Judge relentlessly: the play-by-play shows Judge drew multiple walks off Lopez and was intentionally avoided in key spots, producing only 1 total base (an RBI single per the ESPN box score showing 'Judge 1-2' with RISP). The cruelest moment came in the 5th inning of the May 30 game (wrong game — confirmed May 31 data shows Judge went 1-for-2 with a walk and an RBI, totaling exactly 1 base), where a deep fly to right off Ginn was caught at the wall — described as 'a home run in 16 ballparks, including Yankee Stadium.' Judge hit the ball hard, got on base multiple times, and drove in a run, but the A's strategy of issuing walks and one loud out at the warning track held him to exactly the 1.5 line threshold.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a single-game prop outcome driven by opponent walk strategy and one warning-track out, not a systematic model failure.

picks.headline: Nationals get plus-money value at home against a struggling Griffin Canning.
picks.the_thesis:
Griffin Canning has been a disaster in 2026 — 7.54 ERA, 1.54 WHIP, and a 13.1% walk rate in just 22.2 IP. He can't throw strikes, and when he does, he gets hit hard. The Nationals' offense has been solid at 5.42 R/G, and they should capitalize on Canning's control issues.

Zack Littell isn't an ace (5.23 ERA, 2.54 HR/9), but he's been serviceable and the Nationals have home-field advantage. The market has moved toward Washington — they opened as slight underdogs and are now -102 favorites at FanDuel. That 4.1 percentage point move since open suggests sharp money likes the home side.

The total has also climbed from 9.0 to 9.5, indicating the market expects runs. In a high-scoring game, the team with the better offense (Washington at 5.42 R/G vs. San Diego at 3.95 R/G) has the edge.
picks.the_market: Nationals opened as slight underdogs and have moved to -102 favorites. Total climbed from 9.0 to 9.5. Sharp money on Washington.
picks.weather_park: Nationals Park, 70°F, wind 4 mph L-to-R. Neutral park (1.01 runs PF).
picks.case_against: Littell's 2.54 HR/9 is alarming, and the Padres have power hitters who can exploit that. If Canning finds his command early, this could flip. San Diego's bullpen is also solid.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Canning walking himself into trouble, Washington's offense capitalizing on free baserunners, and the home team holding serve in a high-scoring game.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Canning's walk rate is criminal. The Nationals just have to show up.

picks.headline: Two quality arms, two weak offenses — 7.0 is the right number, but the under has edge.
picks.the_thesis:
Ranger Suarez has been Boston's most reliable starter in 2026, posting a 3.02 ERA and 1.08 WHIP through 53.2 IP. He's a ground-ball pitcher who limits hard contact, and Progressive Field's neutral-to-pitcher-friendly profile (0.97 runs PF) suits his style. Cleveland's offense has been anemic — 4.08 R/G is bottom-10 in baseball.

Tanner Bibee on the other side has struggled (4.57 ERA, 1.71 HR/9), but Boston's offense hasn't been much better at 3.89 R/G. This is a game where both teams will scratch out runs but neither will explode. The total opened at 7.33 and has settled at 7.0, suggesting sharp money has already pushed this down.

Both bullpens are solid, and the 5:40 PM ET start means we avoid the late-game fatigue that can lead to crooked innings.
picks.the_market: Total opened 7.33 and has dropped to 7.0 — sharp money on the under. Line movement supports our side.
picks.weather_park: Progressive Field, 74°F, wind 6 mph R-to-L. Neutral conditions.
picks.case_against: Bibee's HR/9 is elevated, and if Boston's power hitters connect, this could go over quickly. Suarez's 8.0% BB rate could also put runners on base for Cleveland's lineup to capitalize.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Suarez going 6+ quality innings, Cleveland's offense continuing to struggle, and both teams playing small ball rather than hitting for power.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Two offenses that can't score, two pitchers who can get outs. Math says under.

autopsy.post_mortem: The game was actually alive for the under through six innings — Bibee held a 4-3 lead and was on track to exit with the score well within range. But the thesis had a critical blind spot: Bibee entered this start having surrendered a franchise record-tying five home runs in three innings against Washington his previous outing, and the Boston Globe's pre-game notes flagged that Suarez had a bimodal pattern — either dominant or shelled, with four-plus runs allowed in four of his ten starts. The under died in the seventh when Boston sent 10 batters to the plate against three Cleveland relievers, scoring six runs to turn a 4-3 deficit into a 9-4 final. The 'both bullpens are solid' assumption was the kill shot — Cleveland's relief corps collapsed at the exact moment the game needed them most.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Before backing an under involving Bibee, require his last-start HR/9 and hard-hit rate to be within 1.0 of his season average — a single blowup start is a warning sign of mechanical issues that carry over; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor Bibee under picks for pattern over next 4-6 starts.

picks.headline: Strider's 31% K rate meets a Reds lineup that whiffs — 6.5 is too low.
picks.the_thesis:
Spencer Strider has found his groove since returning from the IL. Over his last three starts (17.2 IP), he's struck out 21 batters while posting a 2.04 ERA. His 31.1% K rate on the season is elite, and he's been particularly effective at generating whiffs on his changeup (69.2% whiff rate) and working the lower quadrant against lefties.

The Reds present an ideal matchup. Cincinnati's lineup features several high-strikeout hitters, and Strider's ability to miss bats should translate to a big K total. The line at 6.5 feels low given his recent form — he's averaged 7 K's per start since returning.

Great American Ball Park is hitter-friendly, but that actually helps the K prop — Strider will likely pitch deeper into the game with run support, giving him more opportunities to rack up strikeouts. The wind blowing in from LF today neutralizes some of the park's HR-boosting tendencies.
picks.the_market: Line set at 6.5 with over juiced to -136. Market respects Strider's K upside but hasn't pushed the line to 7.5 yet. Value on the over given his recent form.
picks.weather_park: Great American Ball Park is hitter-friendly (1.06 runs PF), but wind blowing in from LF today. 73°F, partly cloudy.
picks.case_against: Strider's fastball velocity has been down (95.3 mph vs. 97+ in 2023), and his walk rate has been elevated (14.6% BB rate). If he can't locate early, he may not go deep enough to hit 7 K's. The Reds could also stack lefties against him.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Strider going 5-6 innings with his usual swing-and-miss stuff, the Reds' lineup chasing his changeup and slider, and a clean enough outing to reach 7+ strikeouts.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Strider's changeup is unhittable right now. The Reds are going to swing through a lot of pitches.

picks.headline: Bryce Miller at T-Mobile with 8 mph wind blowing in — textbook under spot.
picks.the_thesis:
This is a convergence of factors that all point the same direction. Bryce Miller has been excellent since returning from the IL — his 2.25 ERA and 3.2% walk rate in limited innings show elite command. T-Mobile Park is already one of baseball's most pitcher-friendly venues (0.93 runs PF, 0.93 HR PF), and today's 8 mph wind blowing in from left field suppresses HR by an additional 10%.

Merrill Kelly on the other side has struggled (5.25 ERA, 1.42 WHIP), but the Mariners' offense isn't built to exploit that — they're averaging just 4.27 R/G. This is a game where both teams will struggle to score, and the under at 7.5 offers value.

The market has held this line steady at 7.5 since open, suggesting sharp money agrees with the under thesis. Both bullpens are solid, and the late start time (8:10 PM local) means cooler temperatures that further suppress offense.

picks.headline: Elite SP mismatch — Misiorowski's 40% K rate meets Imai's 17% walk rate.
picks.the_thesis:
Jacob Misiorowski has emerged as one of baseball's most dominant pitchers in 2026. His 40.3% strikeout rate leads all qualified starters, and his 1.83 ERA through 64 IP reflects genuine dominance rather than luck — his fastball has touched 103.2 mph and he's generating whiffs at an elite rate. The Brewers ace faces Tatsuya Imai, who has been a disaster in his rookie season: 6.17 ERA, 17.5% walk rate, and 1.56 HR/9 in just 23.1 IP.

The matchup profile couldn't be cleaner. Imai's control issues (17.5% BB rate is worst among qualified starters) will put runners on base constantly, and the Astros' 4.52 R/G offense isn't equipped to keep pace with Milwaukee's lineup against a pitcher who can't throw strikes. The Brewers have the superior bullpen depth to close this out.

The market agrees — Milwaukee opened around -190 and has held steady at -193, with the run line sitting at -111. That's value for a team with this kind of pitching edge. The roof will be closed at Daikin Park, neutralizing any weather variance.

picks.the_market: Brewers opened -190 and have held at -193. The run line at -111 offers value given the SP mismatch. No significant line movement — market is comfortable with Milwaukee here.
picks.weather_park: Roof closed at Daikin Park — neutral conditions, no weather impact.
picks.case_against: Misiorowski has dealt with cramping issues in two of his last three starts, exiting early both times. If he can't go deep, the Brewers' bullpen will be tested. The Astros are also a veteran team that can rally late at home.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Misiorowski going 6+ innings of dominant ball, Imai walking himself into trouble early, and the Brewers' offense capitalizing on free baserunners.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Imai's walk rate is higher than some pitchers' K rates. The Brewers are going to feast.

picks.headline: SGA's 31.1 PPG baseline meets a must-win Game 7 at home.
picks.the_thesis:
Shai Gilgeous-Alexander has been the Thunder's engine all season, averaging 31.1 points per game — second in the NBA. In a Game 7 at home, with a trip to the Finals on the line, he's going to have the ball in his hands for every meaningful possession. The Thunder's offense runs through him, and there's no reason to expect that to change tonight.

The Spurs have been solid defensively (111.5 points allowed per game, 8th in the league), but they don't have a lockdown perimeter defender who can contain SGA. Stephon Castle has been good, but SGA is a different animal. He's going to get his shots, and he's going to make them.

The line is set at 30.5, and the over is getting -114 at DraftKings. The market implied probability is roughly 53.3%. I'm pricing this at 57% — that's a 3.7 cent edge. It's a thinner edge than the Wembanyama play, but the floor is high because SGA is so reliable.

picks.headline: Wembanyama's 11.5 RPG baseline meets Game 7 intensity and extended minutes.
picks.the_thesis:
This is a Western Conference Finals Game 7. Victor Wembanyama has been the best player in this series, averaging 28.2 points per game and leading the Spurs in rebounds at 11.5 per game during the regular season. In Game 6, he dropped 28 points in 28 minutes in a blowout win — but Game 7 will be different. This will be a close, grinding game, and Wembanyama will play 35+ minutes.

The Thunder have been outrebounded by 1.1 boards per game on average this season. They don't have a dominant rebounder to match Wembanyama's length and athleticism. Chet Holmgren is their best option, but he's averaging 8.5 rebounds per game — that's not enough to contain Wemby on the glass.

The line is set at 12.5, and the over is getting -108 at DraftKings. The market implied probability is roughly 51.9%. I'm pricing this at 59% — that's a 7+ cent edge. In a Game 7, Wembanyama will be on the floor for every meaningful minute, and the boards will be there.
picks.the_market: The line is set at 12.5 across all books. The over is getting -108 at DraftKings, -108 at FanDuel, -105 at BetMGM. No significant movement.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Paycom Center. No weather concerns.
picks.case_against: If the Spurs get blown out early, Wembanyama's minutes could be limited. The Thunder could also crash the glass harder in a Game 7, limiting Wemby's opportunities. And 12.5 is above his season average — he needs to exceed his baseline.
picks.what_were_betting_on: A close Game 7 with Wembanyama playing 35+ minutes and dominating the glass against a Thunder team that doesn't have the personnel to match his length.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Game 7. Wemby. Boards. The math is simple.

picks.headline: Bryan Woo's elite control in a pitcher's paradise against a HR-prone Ryne Nelson.
picks.the_thesis:
T-Mobile Park is one of the best pitcher's parks in baseball — 0.93 runs PF, 0.93 HR PF. It suppresses offense, especially the long ball. Bryan Woo is the perfect pitcher for this environment. His 1.04 WHIP is elite, his 5.5% BB rate is the best on the Mariners staff, and his 3.82 ERA is solid. He's not a strikeout artist (23.0% K rate), but he doesn't need to be — he pitches to contact and lets the park do the work.

Ryne Nelson has been struggling. His 4.65 ERA is backed by a 1.65 HR/9 — that's a lot of home runs. But here's the thing: T-Mobile Park suppresses HRs. Nelson's HR-prone profile is less dangerous in Seattle than it would be anywhere else. The total is set at 7, and the under is getting -115 at FanDuel.

The market implied probability on the under is roughly 53.5%. I'm pricing this at 60% — that's a 6.5 cent edge. Both offenses are middling (Seattle 4.26 R/G, Arizona 4.66 R/G), and the park will do the heavy lifting.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 7 and hasn't moved. The under is getting -115 at FanDuel, -120 at BetMGM, -122 at DraftKings. The market is comfortable with this number.
picks.weather_park: T-Mobile Park is outdoors but plays as one of the most pitcher-friendly parks in baseball. No weather concerns.
picks.case_against: If Woo doesn't have his command and walks a few, Arizona's lineup has enough pop to capitalize. Nelson could also have a good start — his stuff plays, he's just been unlucky with the long ball. A 7 total is already low.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Woo going 6+ innings with minimal damage, Nelson keeping it close despite his HR-prone profile, and the park suppressing offense.
picks.scott_bot_quip: T-Mobile Park is where home runs go to die. We're betting on the graveyard.

picks.headline: Elite lefty Messick at home against a cold Boston offense in a pitcher-friendly park.
picks.the_thesis:
Parker Messick has been Cleveland's best starter this season, and it's not particularly close. His 2.24 ERA is backed by a 1.04 WHIP and a 28.0% K rate — that's elite strikeout stuff with pinpoint control (7.6% BB rate). In his last start against Philadelphia, he threw 5.2 shutout innings and lowered his ERA even further. He's not getting lucky; he's pitching like an ace.

Boston's offense has been anemic, scoring just 3.8 runs per game on the season. Sonny Gray is a solid veteran arm (3.27 ERA, 1.20 WHIP), but he's been limited to about 14.67 IP per start in his last three outings — that's a shorter leash than you'd want for an over. Progressive Field plays neutral-to-pitcher-friendly (0.97 runs PF, 0.96 HR PF), and the total is set at 6.5 with the under getting -116 at DraftKings.

The market implied probability on the under is roughly 53.7%. I'm pricing this at 61% true probability — that's a 7+ cent edge. Both starters have the stuff to go deep, both bullpens are competent, and neither offense has been lighting it up. This is a grind-it-out game.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 6.5 and hasn't moved. The under is getting -116 at DraftKings, -118 at FanDuel, -120 at BetMGM. No sharp movement either direction — this is a market that's comfortable with the number.
picks.weather_park: Progressive Field is outdoors but plays neutral-to-pitcher-friendly. No weather concerns tonight.
picks.case_against: If Messick doesn't have his command early and walks a few, Boston's lineup has enough pop to capitalize. Gray's shorter outings mean more bullpen exposure, and Cleveland's pen has been inconsistent. A 6.5 total is already low — there's not much margin for error.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Messick going 6+ innings with 6+ strikeouts, Gray keeping it close, and both bullpens holding serve in a 3-2 or 4-2 type game.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Messick's dealing, Boston's not hitting, and 6.5 is a number we can live with.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis was sound — Messick (2.24 ERA, 1.04 WHIP) vs. a Boston offense ranked 29th in runs scored looked like a textbook under setup. But Messick got lit up in what became his worst start of the season, while Sonny Gray — the 'solid but short' arm we were worried about — turned in six innings of one-run ball. Per ESPN, 'Connor Wong and Jarren Duran drove in three runs apiece' as Boston rolled to a 9-1 final. The box score shows Messick was pulled early (only 93 pitches logged before the bullpen took over), and the Cleveland pen surrendered a cascade of runs via a Ramirez error, a Fairchild throwing error, a Rocchio error, and a Jarren Duran three-run homer that blew the game open. The under was dead by the middle innings — this wasn't a late-game collapse, it was Messick's outlier bad start (his 12th of the season, against a team he'd only faced once before and allowed 9 hits in 3.2 IP in that prior outing) combined with a multi-error defensive meltdown behind him.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Messick's one prior career start vs. BOS (3 runs, 9 hits in 3.2 IP) was a known data point in the case-against but was underweighted — worth monitoring whether his splits vs. AL East contact-heavy lineups diverge from his overall numbers, but a single-game blowout is noise.

picks.headline: The best contact hitter in baseball faces a struggling pitcher at Coors Field.
picks.the_thesis:
Luis Arraez is the best pure contact hitter in baseball. He's won multiple batting titles and rarely strikes out. Michael Lorenzen has been terrible this season — 7.21 ERA, 1.90 WHIP, and a 15.7% K rate. He's not missing bats, and Arraez doesn't swing and miss.

Coors Field is the most hitter-friendly park in baseball (1.18 runs PF), and Arraez's contact-oriented approach plays perfectly here. The thin air means balls carry, and Arraez's line-drive swing generates plenty of hard contact. His hits line is set at 1.5 at -110 on BetMGM — we need two hits, which is well within his range against a pitcher who can't miss bats.

The data requirements are met: Lorenzen's WHIP (1.90) is terrible, his BAA is elevated, and Coors Field boosts all offensive stats. Arraez is a high-floor play in a premium spot.
picks.the_market: Arraez's hits line is -110 over at BetMGM, -116 at DraftKings. The market is pricing in Coors but not fully respecting Lorenzen's struggles.
picks.weather_park: Coors Field, 65°F, clear — the most hitter-friendly park in baseball.
picks.case_against: Arraez could go 1-for-4 with a walk and a hard-hit out. Lorenzen could also get pulled early, limiting ABs. Coors is unpredictable — sometimes pitchers figure it out.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Arraez getting 4-5 ABs, making contact, and the park doing the rest.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Arraez doesn't strike out. Lorenzen can't miss bats. At Coors. Do the math.
