picks.executive_summary: Friday's board is headlined by Carolina's chance to close out Montreal in Game 5 of the Eastern Conference Final — the Hurricanes have won three straight and Frederik Andersen has been the best goalie in the playoffs. On the MLB side, Zack Wheeler returns to ace form against the Dodgers, the Mets-Marlins under features two quality starters at a pitcher-friendly park, and the Seattle-Arizona under at T-Mobile Park offers a clean spot with George Kirby on the mound. The ladder rides with the Hurricanes puck line at +110 — best floor on the board, backed by dominant defensive metrics and home ice.
picks.slate_analysis:
Friday's slate features 17 MLB games and one NHL Eastern Conference Final matchup. The MLB board is pricing-heavy with several favorites in the -140 to -165 range, which limits straight-bet opportunities under our -150 cap. The NHL game is the marquee event: Carolina leads Montreal 3-1 and can clinch at home.

Top candidates that rose during my review: (1) Carolina -1.5 at +110 — the Hurricanes have been historically dominant defensively, allowing just 10 goals in 12 playoff games, and Andersen has a 1.44 GAA this postseason. (2) Mets-Marlins Under 7.5 — Freddy Peralta vs. Max Meyer at Citi Field, two quality arms in a pitcher-friendly park. (3) Seattle-Arizona Under 7.0 — George Kirby at T-Mobile Park against a struggling Zac Gallen. (4) Phillies-Dodgers Under 8.0 — Zack Wheeler has been elite since returning (1.67 ERA in six starts), facing a Dodgers lineup that's potent but Wheeler has the stuff to suppress. (5) Matt Olson Total Bases Over 1.5 at -105 — facing Chris Paddack (6.86 ERA, 1.34 HR/9) at Great American Ball Park, a HR-boosting venue. (6) Luis Arraez Hits Over 1.5 at -110 — Arraez is a contact machine facing Michael Lorenzen (7.21 ERA, 1.90 WHIP) at Coors Field.

What got cut: Tampa Bay ML at -162 exceeds our juice cap. Cleveland ML at -124 has edge but the opposing SP (Samaniego) has only 17.1 IP — sample too thin for confidence. The Coors Field over at 10.5 is tempting but both offenses are bottom-half in R/G, and the market has already priced in the park factor. The Cubs-Cardinals total at 8.0 is a coin flip with no clear edge. Several HR props lacked the opposing SP HR/9 data needed for validation.

picks.headline: Olson faces a struggling Paddack at the most HR-friendly park in baseball.
picks.the_thesis:
Chris Paddack has been brutal this season — 6.86 ERA, 1.67 WHIP, and a 1.34 HR/9. He's regressing hard, with a 7.63 ERA over his last three starts. Matt Olson is a lefty masher who thrives against struggling right-handers, and Great American Ball Park is the most HR-friendly venue in baseball (1.18 HR PF).

Olson's total bases line is set at 1.5 at -105 on DraftKings. That means we need him to record 2+ total bases — a double, two singles, or a home run. Given Paddack's HR/9 and the park factor, a home run is very much in play. Olson has been solid this year, and this is a premium matchup.

The data requirements are met: Paddack's HR/9 (1.34) is elevated, his L3 ERA (7.63) is terrible, and the park factor (1.18 HR) is the highest in baseball. This is a clean prop spot.
picks.the_market: Olson's TB line is -105 over at DraftKings, -125 at BetMGM. The market is respecting the matchup but not fully pricing in Paddack's struggles.
picks.weather_park: Great American Ball Park, 75°F, wind out to left — HR-boosting conditions.
picks.case_against: Olson has been streaky this year, and one bad AB could sink this. Paddack could also get pulled early if he struggles, limiting Olson's at-bats against him.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Olson getting 3-4 ABs against Paddack, making hard contact, and the park doing the rest.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Paddack's serving meatballs at the most hitter-friendly park in baseball. Olson's hungry.

picks.headline: Zack Wheeler has been elite since returning — this total is too high for a neutral park.
picks.the_thesis:
Zack Wheeler has been the best story in baseball since returning from thoracic outlet surgery. In six starts, he's posted a 1.67 ERA with quality starts in five of them. His most recent outing was six scoreless innings against Cleveland, one of the best lineups in the AL. Wheeler's stuff is back — his fastball is sitting 94-95 mph, and he's generating whiffs at an elite rate.

Justin Wrobleski has been solid for the Dodgers (3.07 ERA, 1.11 WHIP), though his K rate is low (13.7%). He's a contact manager who relies on his defense, which works at Dodger Stadium. The Dodgers offense is potent (5.32 R/G), but Wheeler has the stuff to suppress them.

Dodger Stadium is neutral (0.97 runs PF, 0.99 HR PF), so we're not getting park help — but we don't need it. Wheeler is pitching like an ace, and Wrobleski has been good enough to keep games close. The 8.0 at -103 on DraftKings is the play.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.0-8.5 and has settled at 8.0 on DraftKings. The under is getting slight action, but the line hasn't moved. Wheeler's return has been well-publicized, so the market knows he's back.
picks.weather_park: Dodger Stadium, 70°F, clear — neutral park, no weather concerns.
picks.case_against: The Dodgers have the best offense in baseball (5.32 R/G), and if Wheeler has one bad inning, this total is in trouble. Wrobleski's low K rate means balls in play, which can lead to crooked innings.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Wheeler continuing his elite run, Wrobleski keeping it close, and both bullpens holding late.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Wheeler's back, and he's pitching like he never left. The Dodgers are good, but so is he.

picks.headline: George Kirby at T-Mobile Park is the cleanest under spot on the board.
picks.the_thesis:
George Kirby has been excellent this season — 3.54 ERA, 1.21 WHIP, and an elite 5.8% BB rate. He's averaging 22.73 IP over his last three starts, meaning he's going deep into games and limiting bullpen exposure. T-Mobile Park is one of the most pitcher-friendly venues in baseball (0.93 runs PF, 0.93 HR PF), and Kirby's ground-ball tendencies play perfectly here.

Zac Gallen has struggled (4.80 ERA, 1.44 WHIP), but he's still capable of eating innings and keeping games close. Arizona's offense (4.64 R/G) is solid, but they're facing a pitcher who doesn't walk anyone and limits hard contact. Seattle's offense (4.21 R/G) is average, and Gallen has enough stuff to keep them in check.

The 7.0 total at -101 on DraftKings is the best number on the board. FanDuel has 7.5 at +102 over, which tells you the market is split — we're taking the lower number at near-even money.

the_data.label: Arizona R/G
the_data.context: Above average but facing elite arm

picks.the_market: Total is split between 7.0 and 7.5 across books. DraftKings has 7.0 at -101 under, which is the sharpest number. The line hasn't moved, suggesting balanced action.
picks.weather_park: T-Mobile Park, outdoor but covered — pitcher-friendly venue with HR-suppressing dimensions.
picks.case_against: Gallen has been hittable this year (4.80 ERA), and if Seattle gets to him early, the over comes into play. Arizona's offense has pop, and one crooked inning could blow this up.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Kirby going 7+ innings with minimal damage, Gallen keeping it close, and the park suppressing any fly-ball power.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Kirby doesn't walk anyone, and T-Mobile doesn't let balls fly. Math is math.

picks.headline: Two quality starters at a pitcher-friendly park — this total is too high.
picks.the_thesis:
Freddy Peralta and Max Meyer are both pitching well, and Citi Field suppresses offense. Peralta has a 3.52 ERA with a 24% K rate, while Meyer has been even better — 2.52 ERA, 1.05 WHIP, and a 27.2% K rate. Both starters have the stuff to go deep into games and limit damage.

Citi Field has a 0.97 runs park factor and a 0.91 HR park factor — it's one of the more pitcher-friendly venues in baseball. The Mets offense has been mediocre (3.82 R/G), and while Miami has been slightly better (4.23 R/G), they're facing a quality arm in Peralta who can miss bats.

The market has this at 7.5, which feels like a half-run too high given the pitching matchup and venue. Both starters have BB/9 rates under 3.5 (Peralta 10.3% BB rate, Meyer 8.8%), so we're not worried about free passes inflating the score.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has held. The under is getting slight action at -118, but the line hasn't moved. This feels like a spot where the market is respecting both arms.
picks.weather_park: Citi Field, 72°F, mild conditions — park factor is the story here, not weather.
picks.case_against: Peralta's BB rate (10.3%) is borderline concerning — if he walks the ballpark, Miami could string together a crooked inning. Meyer has only 60.2 IP this season, so there's some sample size risk.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings, limited traffic on the bases, and the park suppressing any fly-ball damage.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Two arms, one pitcher's park, and two offenses that couldn't hit water if they fell out of a boat.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis leaned on Meyer's ERA and Peralta's K-rate while ignoring Peralta's known Marlins-specific vulnerability (career 4.18 ERA vs. MIA) and his 8 HR allowed in 61.1 IP — Liam Hicks had 2 HR in 6 prior BvP PA against him and delivered again. Meyer himself got torched in a 4-run Mets inning (Bichette single → Soto walk → Young walk → Ewing 2-RBI single → Baty 2-RBI single), and a depleted Mets bullpen (missing Senga, Burdi, Herget, and others) had no margin to hold the game once Peralta exited. The final 9-7 (16 total) wasn't a fluke — the structural conditions for a high-scoring game were present in the pre-game data and were underweighted.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a starter has a HR/9 above 1.10 AND a documented negative BvP split against the opposing lineup's cleanup hitter (2+ HR in ≤8 PA), flag the Under as degraded regardless of ERA headline — but validate against a larger sample (N=1 here, insufficient to establish rule).
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Pattern to watch: Under picks where one starter has a sub-1.30 ERA but a 1.10+ HR/9 rate and a depleted home bullpen behind them.

picks.headline: Carolina can clinch at home with the best defense and goaltending in the playoffs.
picks.the_thesis:
The Hurricanes have been historically dominant this postseason. They swept both the Senators and Flyers in the first two rounds, then took a 3-1 series lead over Montreal with a 4-0 shutout in Game 4. Frederik Andersen has been the best goalie in the playoffs — his 1.44 GAA leads all netminders, and he's posted three shutouts already. Carolina's defensive structure has been suffocating: they've allowed just 10 goals in 12 games, the fewest by any team this deep into a playoff run since the 1985 Oilers.

Montreal is gassed. The Canadiens needed seven games to beat both Tampa Bay and Buffalo just to get here, and they've been outplayed in three straight games. Their offense has generated just 18 shots on goal in the last two games combined. Jakub Dobes has been excellent, but he can't steal games when his team can't create chances. The Hurricanes are 6-0 on the road this postseason and now return home with a chance to clinch.

The -1.5 puck line at +110 offers tremendous value. Carolina has won by 2+ goals in 8 of their 12 playoff games. With home ice, a rested roster, and the best goaltender in the tournament, this is the highest-floor play on the board.
picks.the_market: Carolina opened around -230 ML and has held steady. The puck line at +110 offers plus-money on a team that's covered it in two-thirds of their playoff games. Sharp money has been on the under, but the spread has seen balanced action.
picks.weather_park: Indoor arena — no weather impact.
picks.case_against: Montreal stole Game 1 on the road and has shown resilience all postseason. Elimination games bring desperation, and Dobes has the ability to steal a game if Carolina gets complacent. A 3-2 Carolina win would be a push.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Carolina's defensive structure holding, Andersen continuing his elite run, and the Hurricanes' offense generating enough to win by 2+ goals at home.
picks.scott_bot_quip: The Canes have been a buzzsaw. Montreal's just hoping the blade dulls.
picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board — elite defense, best goalie in the playoffs, home ice, clinch game. The +110 price on a team that's covered -1.5 in 67% of their playoff games is the definition of a high-floor ladder spot.

picks.headline: SGA has been carrying OKC with Jalen Williams out — expect another big scoring night.
picks.the_thesis:
Shai Gilgeous-Alexander has been the Thunder's entire offense with Jalen Williams sidelined. Williams has missed Games 3, 4, and 5 with a left hamstring strain, and SGA has stepped up in a massive way. He's been the primary scorer, playmaker, and closer for OKC.

The Thunder still hold a 3-2 series lead despite being shorthanded, and that's largely because of SGA's brilliance. In a potential closeout game on the road, he'll need to be even more aggressive. The Spurs will throw everything at him defensively, but SGA has shown he can score against anyone.

The line is set at 29.5, which is right around his playoff average. But in a closeout game with his team shorthanded, expect SGA to take 25+ shots and push for 30+.
picks.the_market: Line is 29.5 at DraftKings (-104), 30.5 at BetMGM (+115). We're taking the better number at DK.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game. No weather impact.
picks.case_against: The Spurs defense has been solid on SGA this series. If they continue to contain him and force role players to beat them, he could fall short. Elimination games are unpredictable.
picks.what_were_betting_on: SGA taking 25+ shots in a closeout game, being aggressive from the opening tip, and the Thunder needing him to carry the load with Williams out.
picks.scott_bot_quip: SGA's been carrying OKC without Williams. In a closeout game? He's not stopping now.
picks.slate_assessment: Light 6-game MLB slate plus a Western Conference Finals elimination game. Chris Sale vs. Payton Tolle at Fenway and Paul Skenes vs. Colin Rea at PNC Park headline the pitching matchups. Spurs host Thunder in Game 6 with San Antonio facing elimination.
picks.executive_summary: Thursday's board is thin but sharp. Six MLB games and a Western Conference Finals elimination game give us enough to work with. The Skenes-Rea matchup at PNC Park is the cleanest spot on the slate — reigning Cy Young winner at home in a pitcher's park against a struggling Colin Rea. Chris Sale continues his dominant run against a Boston lineup that's been anemic. The NBA game features a desperate Spurs team at home with Wembanyama due for a bounce-back after a 4-for-15 shooting night. The ladder rides with the Spurs spread — best floor on the board with San Antonio 20-6 SU coming off losses this season.
picks.slate_analysis:
The board today is a 6-game MLB slate plus one NBA Western Conference Finals elimination game. No NHL, no other NBA. The MLB games are spread across the afternoon and evening, with first pitches ranging from 1:10 PM ET to 8:05 PM ET. The NBA tips at 8:30 PM ET. Pricing is relatively tight across the board — no massive favorites, no obvious traps.

picks.headline: Bounce-back spot for Wemby after a 4-for-15 shooting night in Game 5.
picks.the_thesis:
Victor Wembanyama is averaging 28.2 points per game in this series. His scoring line reads 41, 24, 25, 33, and 20 — and the drop to 20 points in Game 5 came on only 15 field goal attempts, his lowest shot volume of the series. That's not sustainable for a player of his caliber in an elimination game.

The historical data is compelling: in the last 21 games where Wembanyama took 15 or fewer field goal attempts without injury impact, he averaged 28 points in the following game and topped 30 in nine of those outings. The Spurs coaching staff has been clear — 'He has to take more than 15 shots,' said coach Mitch Johnson. 'We need him to be aggressive,' pleaded Stephon Castle.

This is an elimination game at home. Wembanyama will be aggressive. He'll take 20+ shots. And with his efficiency (he's shooting well from the line and has shown he can score in bunches), 28+ points is well within reach.
picks.the_market: Line is set at 27.5 across books. The market is pricing in a bounce-back but not fully.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game. No weather impact.
picks.case_against: The Thunder defense has been elite this series. If they continue to limit Wemby's touches, he could have another quiet night. Foul trouble is always a risk.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Wembanyama taking 20+ shots, being aggressive from the opening tip, and the Spurs feeding him early and often in an elimination game.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Wemby shot 4-for-15 and his coach said 'he has to take more shots.' Message received.

picks.headline: Two starters who can go deep in a pitcher-friendly park.
picks.the_thesis:
This is a classic under spot. Paul Skenes has a 3.9 BB% — that's elite command that keeps runners off base. Colin Rea's 7.3 BB% is higher but still under our 3.5 BB/9 threshold for unders. Both pitchers can go 6+ innings, which limits bullpen exposure.

PNC Park is one of the best pitcher's parks in baseball. The 0.95 runs park factor and 0.91 HR factor mean balls don't carry and runs are suppressed. The Cubs offense (4.73 R/G) is solid but not elite, and they're facing the best pitcher in baseball. The Pirates offense (4.89 R/G) is better but facing a pitcher who can limit damage.

The total is set at 7.5, which feels about right for two average offenses. But with Skenes on the mound and the park factors working in our favor, we're getting enough edge to play the under.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has held across all books. No movement suggesting sharp money on either side.
picks.weather_park: PNC Park suppresses runs and HRs. Outdoor game, mild conditions expected.
picks.case_against: Skenes has been shaky lately — if he doesn't go deep, the Pirates bullpen could give up runs. The Cubs have some pop in the lineup.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings, limited bullpen exposure, and the park factors suppressing offense.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Two starters who can go deep, a park that eats home runs, and a total that's begging for the under.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis leaned entirely on Skenes suppressing the Cubs, but the pick ignored two compounding red flags that were publicly available pre-game: Skenes had allowed 9 earned runs across his prior two starts (Phillies and Blue Jays), was entering what would become his first-ever three-game losing streak, and his pitch count was already at 70 through just four innings on Thursday. The game unraveled in the 5th-6th: Skenes walked Busch, surrendered an infield single and a bloop single to Suzuki that scored the first run, then a Pirates defensive error on an infield grounder by Happ let another run score before Skenes was pulled at 5.1 innings — well short of the '6+ innings' the thesis required. The Cubs bullpen then teed off on a depleted Pirates relief corps (four relievers used the night before), with Ian Happ's two-run homer in the 8th pushing it to 5-2, and Chicago added two more to finish 7-2. The under was never really alive: the 'case against' section of the pick itself flagged Skenes' shakiness and bullpen exposure, but the model still rated confidence at 3 and played the unit — the data didn't support the conviction.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When the named anchor starter has allowed 3+ ER in each of his prior two starts AND is projected for a rising pitch count (70+ through 4 IP in recent outings), discount under confidence by at least one tier and flag bullpen exposure as a primary risk, not a footnote — but validate across a minimum of 15–20 similar starter-fatigue under spots before codifying as a hard filter.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule; this is a directional signal only and requires pattern confirmation across a larger sample of 'ace in mini-slump' under bets.

picks.headline: Elimination game at home with Wembanyama due for a bounce-back after a 4-for-15 shooting night.
picks.the_thesis:
The San Antonio Spurs are facing elimination at home, and Victor Wembanyama is due for a monster game. In Game 5, Wemby shot just 4-for-15 from the field — his worst offensive performance of the series. But here's the key stat: in the last 21 games where Wembanyama took 15 or fewer field goal attempts without injury impact, he averaged 28 points in the following game and topped 30 in nine of those outings.

The Thunder are also shorthanded. Jalen Williams remains questionable after missing Games 3, 4, and 5 with a left hamstring strain. Ajay Mitchell is out. Despite carrying those injuries for most of this series, OKC still leads 3-2 — but they've been doing it with smoke and mirrors.

The Spurs are 20-6 SU and 18-8 ATS when coming off a loss this season. That's a team that responds. At home, facing elimination, with their franchise player due for a bounce-back? This is the spot.
picks.the_market: Spurs opened -3.5 and have held. The market respects the home elimination spot but isn't overreacting to Game 5.
picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Frost Bank Center. No weather impact.
picks.case_against: The Thunder have won this series without Jalen Williams. SGA has been incredible. If Wembanyama doesn't bounce back, OKC can close it out on the road.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Wembanyama coming out aggressive, taking 20+ shots, and the Spurs' home crowd pushing them to a comfortable win that forces Game 7.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Wemby shot 4-for-15 and the Spurs lost by 13. He's not doing that again. Not at home. Not facing elimination.
picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board. San Antonio is 20-6 SU coming off losses, Wembanyama is due for a bounce-back, and the Thunder are missing their second-best player. Elimination games at home favor the desperate team.

picks.headline: Chris Sale's dominant run continues against a Boston offense averaging just 3.85 runs per game.
picks.the_thesis:
Chris Sale is pitching like the 2024 Cy Young winner again. His 1.89 ERA and 0.87 WHIP are elite, and his 29.9 K% shows the stuff is still there at age 37. Sale has been particularly sharp recently — in his last start against the Rockies, he struck out 11 over 7 innings while allowing just one run.

The Boston offense is struggling. They're averaging just 3.85 runs per game this season, which ranks near the bottom of the league. Payton Tolle is a solid young arm (2.45 ERA, 0.82 WHIP), but he's facing an Atlanta offense that's averaging 5.16 R/G — the best in this matchup by a wide margin.

The Braves have the pitching edge (Sale > Tolle), the offensive edge (5.16 R/G vs 3.85 R/G), and the experience edge (Sale in a big spot vs. a young pitcher). The market has this right at -135, but we're getting enough edge to play it.
picks.the_market: Braves opened around -135 and have held steady. No significant line movement. The market respects Sale on the road.
picks.weather_park: Fenway Park is hitter-friendly (1.05 runs PF) but HR-suppressing (0.96 HR PF). Outdoor game, mild conditions.
picks.case_against: Sale is 37 and has had durability issues throughout his career. Tolle has been sharp this year and could match Sale pitch-for-pitch. Fenway is a tough place to pitch.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Sale going 6+ innings with 7+ strikeouts, the Braves offense providing run support against a young pitcher, and Atlanta's bullpen closing it out.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Sale's still got it. Boston's offense doesn't. Sometimes it's that simple.

picks.headline: Home underdog in a must-win Game 4 of the Eastern Conference Finals.
picks.the_thesis:
Montreal is down 2-1 in the Eastern Conference Finals after losing two straight overtime games in Carolina. Tonight they're home at the Bell Centre, where they've been dominant this postseason — they beat Tampa Bay in 7 games and Buffalo in 7 games to get here, with several of those wins coming at home.

The Hurricanes are the better team on paper, but playoff hockey is about momentum and home ice. Montreal won Game 1 decisively (6-2) and has shown they can compete with Carolina. At +130, we're getting 43% implied probability on a team that I think wins closer to 48% of the time.

The key players to watch are Cole Caufield and Nick Suzuki for Montreal, and Andrei Svechnikov and Sebastian Aho for Carolina. The Canadiens' goaltending has been solid, and the Bell Centre crowd will be electric for a must-win game.
picks.the_market: Montreal opened at +126 and has moved to +130 at DraftKings. The public is on Carolina, but the sharps know home ice matters in playoff hockey.
picks.weather_park: Bell Centre, indoor — no weather impact.
picks.case_against: Carolina has been the better team in Games 2-3, winning both in overtime. The Hurricanes' depth and defensive structure have been elite this postseason. Montreal's goaltending could crack under pressure.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Bell Centre crowd lifting Montreal, Caufield and Suzuki finding their scoring touch, and the Canadiens forcing a Game 5 with a 3-2 or 4-3 win.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Two OT losses at home? The Bell Centre owes Montreal one.
picks.slate_assessment: 10-game MLB slate plus NHL Eastern Conference Finals Game 4 — two elite pitching matchups (Mize vs Soriano, Ohtani vs Sugano) and a must-win playoff hockey game anchor the board.
picks.executive_summary: Two aces squaring off at Comerica Park headline tonight's slate — Casey Mize (2.47 ERA, 0.98 WHIP) vs José Soriano (2.44 ERA) in a game where the total has ballooned from 7.5 to 9.0 despite both starters pitching at elite levels. The market is overcorrecting, and the under at 9.0 is the sharpest play on the board. Shohei Ohtani takes the mound against Colorado with a 0.73 ERA — the Dodgers -420 ML is unplayable, but the run line and total offer angles. In the NHL, Carolina leads Montreal 2-1 in the Eastern Conference Finals and the Canadiens are home dogs getting +130 in a must-win Game 4. The ladder rides with the Tigers-Angels under — two elite starters, soft offenses, and a neutral park.
picks.slate_analysis:
Tonight's board features 10 MLB games and one NHL Eastern Conference Finals matchup. The MLB slate is headlined by two pitching duels — Mize vs Soriano in Detroit and Ohtani vs Sugano in Los Angeles — while the NHL offers a pivotal Game 4 between Carolina and Montreal. The pricing climate is soft in spots: several totals have moved significantly from their openers, and the Dodgers are priced at -420 ML, which is unplayable for straight bets.

The top candidates that rose during my review were: (1) Tigers-Angels Under 9.0 — two elite starters with sub-2.50 ERAs, soft offenses, and a neutral park; the total opened at 7.5 and has climbed to 9.0, which feels like an overcorrection; (2) Dodgers-Rockies Under 8.0 — Ohtani's 0.73 ERA against a weak Rockies lineup, though Sugano's 1.52 HR/9 introduces variance; (3) Braves ML at Boston — Bryce Elder (1.97 ERA) vs Connelly Early (3.33 ERA), but the -136 juice is borderline; (4) Yankees-Royals Under 8.5 — Gerrit Cole's first start since returning from Tommy John surgery, wind blowing in at Kauffman, but Cole's 6.0 IP sample is too thin to trust; (5) Montreal Canadiens ML +130 — home underdog in a must-win Game 4, but data confidence is low at 0.35; (6) Ohtani Ks Over 7.5 — facing a weak Rockies lineup, but the line is set at even money and his season K% of 28.4% suggests the market is efficient.

What got cut and why: The Braves ML at -136 exceeds my comfort zone for a road favorite against a lefty at Fenway — the park factor (1.05 runs) and Early's solid 3.33 ERA make this closer to a coin flip than the market suggests. The Yankees-Royals under was tempting with wind blowing in, but Cole has only 6.0 IP this season and his stuff was down in rehab starts (4.66 ERA across 29 rehab innings). The Canadiens ML at +130 was intriguing, but the data confidence of 0.35 is below my 0.6 floor — I can't build a fair price without more granular team metrics. The Ohtani Ks Over 7.5 at even money offers no edge; my fair line is 7.5 exactly.

The final six picks are: (1) Tigers-Angels Under 9.0 — the ladder pick, two elite starters, soft offenses, market overcorrection; (2) Dodgers-Rockies Under 8.0 — Ohtani's dominance against a weak lineup; (3) Pirates ML vs Cubs — Chandler vs Taillon at PNC Park, but the -170 juice is borderline so I'm taking the run line; (4) Braves-Red Sox Under 7.0 — Elder's 1.97 ERA and Fenway's HR-suppressing factor; (5) Mets ML vs Reds — Brazobán's 1.73 ERA at home against Abbott's 3.97 ERA; (6) Cole Ks Under 4.5 — first start back from TJ surgery, limited pitch count expected. The ladder rides with the Tigers-Angels under — best floor on the board with two elite starters and a market that has overcorrected.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis leaned on home-ice momentum and a must-win atmosphere, but Carolina dismantled that narrative in the first period alone — Aho (PP goal at 14:58), Staal (deflection at 16:07), and Stankoven (2-on-1 rush at 17:46) scored three times in a 2:47 span to make it 3-0 before the period was over, and the Bell Centre crowd was dead. Frederik Andersen needed only 18 saves for his third postseason shutout, while Dobes faced 39 shots — the Canadiens were outshot 19-3 in the third period alone, with the crowd audibly chanting 'Shoot the puck!' at their own team. The core model error was treating Game 1's 6-2 blowout as signal that Montreal could 'compete' with Carolina, when in reality Carolina had entered that game flat after an 11-day layoff off a sweep; Games 2-4 showed the true talent gap once the Hurricanes were locked in.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a team's best win in a series came against a rested opponent returning from a long layoff (11+ days), discount that result as a talent signal and weight the subsequent games more heavily before pricing home-dog value — but N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule.

picks.headline: Gerrit Cole's return, wind blowing in, and a total that's dropped from 9.0 to 8.5.
picks.the_thesis:
Gerrit Cole makes his 2026 debut tonight after recovering from Tommy John surgery. His rehab numbers were solid (4.66 ERA, 1.07 WHIP, 28:3 K:BB across 29 innings), and he's facing a Royals team that ranks near the bottom of MLB in runs per game (3.84). Noah Cameron (4.72 ERA, 1.45 WHIP) has been inconsistent, but the Yankees' offense has been potent (4.96 R/G).

The key edge here is the weather — wind is blowing in from LF at 9 mph, suppressing HR by about 12%. Kauffman Stadium already suppresses HR (0.92 PF), and the total has dropped from 9.0 to 8.5. We're getting even money on a line that's moved our way.

The concern is Cole's limited sample — just 6.0 IP this season — and the fact that he'll likely be on a pitch count. But the Royals' offense is soft enough that even a short Cole outing (4-5 IP) should keep the game low.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 9.0 and has dropped to 8.5. We're getting even money on the under at FanDuel — the sharps have already moved this line.

picks.case_against: Cole's 6.0 IP sample is tiny, and he'll likely be on a pitch count (70-80 pitches). If he exits early, the Yankees' bullpen has been inconsistent. Cameron's 4.72 ERA could lead to a crooked inning.

picks.scott_bot_quip: Cole's back, the wind's in, and the sharps already moved this line. We're not early, we're on time.

picks.headline: Brazobán's 1.73 ERA at home against Abbott's 3.97 ERA.
picks.the_thesis:
Huascar Brazobán has been excellent in limited innings this season — 1.73 ERA with a 0.92 WHIP across 26 innings. His 0.35 HR/9 is elite, and he's facing a Reds team that ranks middle-of-the-pack in runs per game (4.5). Andrew Abbott (3.97 ERA, 1.46 WHIP) has been inconsistent, and his 10.5% BB rate creates baserunners.

The Mets are home favorites at -118, which implies about 54% win probability. My fair line is closer to 57%, giving us edge. Citi Field is a pitcher-friendly park (0.97 runs PF, 0.91 HR PF), and the wind blowing out to RF at 11 mph boosts HR by about 16% — but that helps the Mets' power hitters more than the Reds'.

The key concern is Brazobán's limited sample (26 IP) and the fact that he's averaging just 8.67 IP per start in his last 3. If he exits early, the Mets' bullpen will be tested.
picks.the_market: Mets opened as -120 favorites and have held steady. The market is pricing this as a 54% win probability, but the pitching matchup favors the Mets.
picks.weather_park: Citi Field, 80°F, wind 11 mph out to RF — 16% HR boost, but park suppresses HR overall.
picks.case_against: Brazobán's 26 IP sample is small, and his 8.67 IP/start average means the bullpen will be involved early. The Reds have power (Elly De La Cruz, Sal Stewart) that could capitalize on the wind.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Brazobán going 5+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, Mets offense capitalizing on Abbott's walks, and the game finishing 4-2 or 5-3 Mets.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Abbott walks batters. Brazobán doesn't. Math is math.

picks.headline: Bryce Elder's 1.97 ERA meets a cold Red Sox offense at Fenway.
picks.the_thesis:
Bryce Elder has been one of the best pitchers in baseball this season with a 1.97 ERA and 0.99 WHIP across 68.2 innings. He's limiting hard contact and keeping the ball in the park (0.66 HR/9). Tonight he faces a Red Sox team that ranks near the bottom of MLB in runs per game (3.77) and is starting Connelly Early (3.33 ERA, 1.19 WHIP).

Early has been solid but not dominant — his 1.5 HR/9 is concerning at Fenway, but the park's HR factor is actually 0.96 (suppressing). The total has dropped from 8.0 to 7.0-7.5 across books, and we're getting plus money on the under at DraftKings.

The Braves offense is potent (5.25 R/G), but Early has been effective at limiting damage. The key is Elder dominating his half — if he goes 7 innings with 1-2 runs allowed, the under is in great shape even if Early gives up 3-4.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.0 and has dropped to 7.0-7.5 across books. We're getting +104 on the under at DraftKings — plus money on a line that's already moved our way.
picks.weather_park: Fenway Park, 80°F, wind 6 mph out to RF — mild HR boost, but park PF is 0.96 for HR.
picks.case_against: Early's 1.5 HR/9 could lead to a crooked inning against the Braves' power-heavy lineup. Fenway's hitter-friendly reputation could lead to more contact than expected.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Elder going 7+ innings with 1-2 runs allowed, Red Sox offense staying cold, and the game finishing 4-2 or 3-2.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Elder's ERA is lower than the Red Sox's playoff odds.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Elder dominating his half — he didn't come close. Elder was knocked out in 3⅓ innings, surrendering 6 runs on 9 hits (including 2 errors that extended the damage) in a catastrophic 4th inning where BOS sent 9+ batters to the plate and scored 6 runs via singles, errors, and a wild pitch. The under was dead by the 4th inning; this wasn't a late-game variance event. The model over-indexed on Elder's season-long ERA (1.97) without accounting for the risk of a short, blow-up start — a single bad outing from the anchor pitcher destroys the under regardless of how good the opposing pitcher is.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When the under thesis is structurally dependent on one pitcher going deep (6+ IP, ≤2 ER), apply a 'short-start discount' — if that pitcher's start-length variance (e.g. % of starts under 5 IP) exceeds 15%, reduce confidence by at least 1 unit; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — single data point, monitoring for pattern of over-reliance on ERA without start-length floor validation.

picks.the_thesis:

Tonight he faces a Rockies team that ranks near the bottom of MLB in runs per game (4.07) and is starting Tomoyuki Sugano (3.86 ERA, 1.23 WHIP).

Sugano isn't a pushover — he's been solid this season — but his 12.6% K rate is pedestrian and he'll be facing the best offense in baseball (5.35 R/G). The key here is that Ohtani should dominate his half of the game, and the Dodgers' bullpen has been elite. Even if Sugano gives up 4-5 runs, Ohtani limiting the Rockies to 1-2 keeps this under.

Dodger Stadium is a neutral park (0.97 runs PF), and the total hasn't moved from open. The market is pricing this as a 4-4 game, but Ohtani's dominance suggests 3-4 Dodgers runs and 1-2 Rockies runs is the more likely outcome.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.0 and hasn't moved. The market is pricing this as a 4-4 game, but Ohtani's dominance suggests the Rockies side stays low.
picks.weather_park: Dodger Stadium, outdoor but mild conditions — neutral impact.
picks.case_against: Sugano's 1.52 HR/9 could lead to a crooked inning against the Dodgers' power-heavy lineup. If Ohtani is limited after the HBP scare, the Dodgers' bullpen depth is tested.

picks.headline: Two elite starters, soft offenses, and a market that has overcorrected from 7.5 to 9.0.
picks.the_thesis:
Casey Mize and José Soriano are both pitching at elite levels this season, and the market has inexplicably pushed this total from 7.5 at open to 9.0. Mize owns a 2.47 ERA with a 0.98 WHIP and 25.1% K rate — he's been the best version of himself since returning from his adductor strain, posting a 2.43 ERA with a 0.98 WHIP across 43.2 innings. Soriano has been equally dominant with a 2.44 ERA and 27.9% K rate, though his 10.2% BB rate introduces some variance.

The offenses here are soft. Detroit ranks near the bottom of MLB in runs per game (3.87), and the Angels aren't much better at 4.13. Comerica Park is a neutral-to-pitcher-friendly venue with a 0.96 runs park factor, and the wind is blowing left-to-right at 9 mph — not a HR-boosting environment.

The 1.5-run move from open to current is the kind of overcorrection that creates edge. The market is pricing in 9+ runs between two teams that average 8.0 combined against two starters with sub-2.50 ERAs. My fair line is 7.5-8.0, which gives us significant edge at 9.0.
picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has climbed to 9.0 across all three books. This 1.5-run move is unusual for a game featuring two elite starters — the public is chasing runs that aren't coming.
picks.weather_park: Comerica Park, 71°F, wind 9 mph L-to-R — neutral conditions, no HR boost.
picks.case_against: Soriano's 10.2% BB rate could extend innings and put runners on base. If either starter exits early, both bullpens have been shaky. The Angels have scored 5+ runs in 3 of their last 7 games.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings, combined K rate suppressing contact, and the soft offenses failing to capitalize on the few baserunners they get.
picks.scott_bot_quip: The market moved 1.5 runs on two aces. We're not that dumb.
picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board — two elite starters with sub-2.50 ERAs, soft offenses, and a market that has overcorrected by 1.5 runs. This is the kind of play that wins 60%+ of the time.

picks.headline: Dodgers -1.5 at -110 against Kyle Freeland (7.04 ERA) — LAD offense (5.17 R/G) is the best in the NL and the price is near even money.
picks.the_thesis:
The Dodgers are the best offensive team in the National League, scoring 5.17 runs per game — and tonight they face Kyle Freeland, who has a 7.04 ERA, 1.70 WHIP, and 2.13 HR/9 across 38.1 innings. Freeland has been one of the worst starting pitchers in baseball in 2026, and the Dodgers lineup — featuring Shohei Ohtani, Mookie Betts, Freddie Freeman, and Kyle Tucker — is built to punish exactly this type of arm. The run line at -110 on FanDuel is near even money for a team that should win by multiple runs.

Eric Lauer on the other side is also struggling (6.69 ERA, 2.74 HR/9), which creates some over risk. However, the Rockies offense (4.04 R/G) is below average, and Dodger Stadium has a neutral park factor (runs 0.97, HR 0.99). The key edge here is the offensive mismatch — the Dodgers' 5.17 R/G against a 7.04-ERA starter is a recipe for a multi-run win. The run line at -110 implies 52.4% probability — our fair line puts it at 57%, giving us roughly 4.5 cents of edge.

This is a confidence-3 play because Lauer's 2.74 HR/9 creates genuine variance risk — if the Rockies hit a couple of home runs early, the game could tighten. But the Dodgers' offensive floor against Freeland is high enough to make -1.5 at -110 a defensible play.

picks.headline: Ranger Suarez (2.40 ERA, 0.56 HR/9) anchors the under despite wind blowing out at Fenway — the ace suppresses what the park amplifies.
picks.the_thesis:
Ranger Suarez is one of the best pitchers in the American League this season. His 2.40 ERA, 1.01 WHIP, and 0.56 HR/9 represent elite run prevention — the HR/9 is particularly important here because Fenway Park has wind blowing out to center field at 9 mph tonight, boosting HR probability by approximately 13%. Suarez's ability to suppress home runs even in favorable conditions for hitters is the key edge. His 7.4% BB rate is within our under-screening threshold.

Spencer Strider (3.0 ERA) is the concern on the other side. His 14.6% BB rate is elevated and his 1.71 HR/9 is genuinely alarming in a hitter-friendly park with wind out. The Red Sox offense (3.73 R/G) is below average, but Strider's walk rate means he will put runners on base, and Fenway's dimensions could turn a walk into a multi-run inning. However, the market has already priced this in — the total sits at 8.0, not 9.0 or 9.5, suggesting the books are accounting for Suarez's dominance suppressing the Strider-driven over risk.

The under at -108 on FanDuel is the best price. The de-vigged implied probability is approximately 51.9% — our fair line puts it at 57%, giving us roughly 5 cents of edge. This is a confidence-3 play because Strider's walk rate and HR/9 create genuine variance risk, but Suarez's floor is high enough to anchor the under.

picks.case_against: Strider's 14.6% BB rate is the primary risk — walks plus wind-out at Fenway is a dangerous combination. If he issues 3-4 walks and one leaves the yard, the over hits quickly. The Braves offense (5.22 R/G) is one of the best in baseball and could punish Suarez if he has an off night. The wind-out signal is real and the pack flagged it explicitly.

picks.scott_bot_quip: Suarez's HR/9 is 0.56. The wind is blowing out. One ace cancels the other's risk. We'll take the quiet game.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Suarez's elite HR suppression anchoring the under, but the kill shot came from the wrong side of the matchup: Suarez himself collapsed. He sailed through four scoreless innings, then surrendered two runs in the fifth on Matt Olson's 15th homer (a two-run shot that tied it 2-2), and was chased without recording an out in the sixth after Austin Riley tripled, Eli White walked, and Michael Harris II delivered an RBI double — Suarez was charged with five earned runs on six hits over five-plus innings, walking three and hitting a batter, the worst outing of his season. Atlanta's three-run sixth made it 5-2, Harris added a two-run homer in the eighth off the Boston bullpen, and despite three Boston home runs (Duran, Rafaela, Kiner-Falefa), the Red Sox fell 7-6 — 13 combined runs, blowing past the 8.0 total. The model correctly flagged Strider's walk rate as a risk but misidentified which pitcher would blow up: Strider allowed only 3 runs in 5 innings and settled in after the first, while Suarez — the supposed anchor — had his worst start of the year. The thesis was built on the wrong pitcher's floor.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When anchoring an under on a specific starter's ERA/HR suppression, cross-check whether that pitcher has a recent 'cluster' of bad starts masked by a low season ERA — Suarez had allowed 12 of his 13 earned runs in just 13 innings across a subset of starts, a red flag that the ERA was misleading about his true variance profile; flag starters whose ERA and xERA diverge by >0.75 runs for under consideration (N=1, insufficient to establish rule).
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Suarez blowup does not confirm a systematic ERA-masking pattern, but the pre-game data point (12 of 13 ER in 13 innings of bad starts) was available and should have widened the confidence interval on his floor.

picks.headline: Ashcraft (2.89 ERA, 26.4% K%) at pitcher-friendly PNC against a Cubs lineup facing a lefty with thin data — under 8.0 at near-even money.
picks.the_thesis:
Braxton Ashcraft has been one of the most quietly dominant starters in baseball in 2026. His 2.89 ERA, 1.03 WHIP, and 65:16 K:BB across 62.1 innings are backed by a curveball whiff rate that jumped from 36.6% to 50.9% this season — that is a legitimate arsenal upgrade, not a hot streak. He has pitched into the seventh inning in three of his four May starts and has allowed two earned runs or fewer in nine of eleven outings. His 6.5% BB rate is well within our under-screening threshold.

The Cubs send Jordan Wicks to the mound, whose data in the pack is thin (no season stats provided beyond the name), which is a flag — but the market has set this total at 8.0 on DraftKings, suggesting the books are already accounting for a solid Cubs arm. PNC Park is one of the most pitcher-friendly venues in baseball, with a runs park factor of 0.95 and an HR factor of 0.91. Both offenses are middle-of-the-pack (PIT 4.78 R/G, CHC 4.70 R/G), and neither lineup profiles as a high-variance scoring unit.

The under at -108 on DraftKings is the best price on the board for this total. The de-vigged implied probability for the under is approximately 51.9% — our fair line puts it at 60%, giving us roughly 8 cents of edge. The combination of Ashcraft's elite form, a pitcher-friendly park, and two average offenses makes this a clean under play.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on two starters — Ashcraft held up his end completely (1 R, 6.1 IP, quality start), but the Cubs' half of the equation was a ghost. Jordan Wicks was not a real MLB starter: he had a career 5.21 ERA, had spent 2025 exclusively as a reliever with a 6.28 ERA in 14.1 innings, and was recalled from Triple-A Iowa on Sunday — the day before the game — only because Edward Cabrera went on the IL with a finger blister. The pack flagged 'thin data' on Wicks but still priced the under as if a competent starter was taking the mound. He was rocked for 8 runs on 9 hits in 4.1 innings: a Reynolds two-run double and a Valdez 415-foot two-run homer made it 5-0 in the first inning alone, and the game was effectively over before Ashcraft threw his second pitch. Final: PIT 12, CHC 1 — 11 of Pittsburgh's 13 runs came off Wicks and the Cubs' mop-up arms, not Ashcraft.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When the Cubs' (or any team's) listed starter is a same-day recall from Triple-A with a career MLB ERA above 5.00 and no MLB starts in the current season, treat the opposing half of the total as uncapped and do not play unders unless the total is set 1.5+ runs above the ace's expected contribution alone — N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One emergency-callup implosion is noise, but the DATA failure here (no MLB 2026 stats existed for Wicks at pick time, and the pack explicitly flagged it) is a process gap worth tracking.

picks.headline: Kyle Harrison (1.77 ERA, 32.2% K-rate) vs. a Cardinals team that has dropped four of five — and you're getting plus money on the run line.
picks.the_thesis:
Kyle Harrison is the only pitcher in the National League with an ERA below 1.85 and a strikeout rate above 30%. His 1.77 ERA, 59:14 K:BB, and 32.2% strikeout rate across 45.2 innings are not a fluke — his xERA per Statcast sits at 3.43, which is still comfortably a career best and validates the performance. The Brewers' pitching lab has turned Harrison into a weapon: he scrapped his cutter, sharpened his slurve (batters are 2-for-22 against it with 7 Ks), and improved his changeup's vertical drop by 3.4 inches. In his last two starts against the Cubs and Padres, he allowed zero runs across 12 innings with 18 strikeouts and one walk.

The Cardinals enter this game having dropped four of five and facing Harrison for the first time in his career. Michael McGreevy (2.40 ERA, 5.5% BB) is a solid arm, but the Brewers offense (4.92 R/G, best in the NL Central) has the firepower to generate a multi-run cushion. Milwaukee has won six of Harrison's last six starts and is 13-4 in their last 17 games overall.

The run line at +120 on FanDuel is the ladder pick because it lands squarely in the even-money band and Harrison's floor is as high as any arm on the board. You're getting paid to take the team with the better pitcher, the better offense, and the better recent form. The implied probability at +120 is 45.5% — our fair line puts the Brewers winning by 2+ at roughly 53%, giving us nearly 8 cents of edge.

picks.headline: Schlittler (1.50 ERA) vs. Falter (9.82 ERA) — the most lopsided pitching matchup on the board.
picks.the_thesis:
Cam Schlittler is the best pitcher in baseball right now and tonight he faces the worst starting pitcher on the slate. Schlittler enters with a 1.50 ERA, 0.86 WHIP, and a 75:13 K:BB ratio across 66 innings — his xFIP and xERA both sit around 2.60, meaning the underlying metrics validate the surface numbers. He is not a mirage. His 29.6% strikeout rate and 5.1% walk rate create an elite run-prevention profile, and the Royals lineup (22-32, bottom-third offense) profiles as one of the worst matchups he could face. Kansas City's 22% projected strikeout rate against right-handed pitching and average chase rate of 30.2% means they won't punish him with discipline or loud contact.

On the other side, Bailey Falter has a 9.82 ERA and 2.86 WHIP across just 7.1 innings — he is a spot starter who will likely be pulled after two innings in a piggyback arrangement. The Yankees lineup features Aaron Judge (17 HR), Ben Rice (16 HR), and Cody Bellinger, all projected to see multiple at-bats against a pitcher who cannot throw strikes. The run differential in this matchup is extreme. The Yankees have won 12 straight against Kansas City, and tonight's pitching edge is the most pronounced of that entire streak.

The run line at -118 on FanDuel is the value play here. The ML is -200, which is too short for a straight bet under our rules. But -1.5 at -118 is a gift — the Yankees should win by multiple runs far more often than the implied 54% suggests when Schlittler is dealing and the opponent is running out a 9.82-ERA arm. This is the highest-conviction play on the board.

the_data.label: Schlittler ERA / xERA / WHIP

picks.the_market: Yankees ML opened -200 and has held there across all three books. The run line at -118 on FanDuel is the best price available and represents genuine value given the pitching gap. No meaningful line movement — the market has been stable, suggesting sharp money is already priced in.
picks.weather_park: Kauffman Stadium is outdoor; no weather data in pack. Park HR factor 0.92 — slightly suppresses home runs, net neutral for this play.
picks.case_against: Schlittler took a comebacker off his left calf last start and was still limping the next morning per Aaron Boone. If he exits early due to discomfort, the Yankees bullpen faces a Royals lineup that can steal a game against a tired pen. The Yankees have also failed to cover the run line in each of their last seven games following a win, and Kansas City has covered the run line in five straight home games against New York — situational trends cut against the -1.5.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Schlittler goes 6+ innings, Yankees offense scores 4+ runs against Falter and the KC bullpen, final margin of 2+ runs.
picks.scott_bot_quip: A 1.50 ERA vs. a 9.82 ERA. The boys don't need a thesis — they need a receipt.

picks.headline: Petco Park, Vasquez at 2.96, Luzardo's 28% K rate — runs come at a premium tonight.
picks.the_thesis: Petco Park is one of the three most pitcher-friendly parks in baseball (0.93 runs PF, 0.91 HR PF). Randy Vasquez has been a quiet bright spot for the Padres at 2.96 ERA, 1.19 WHIP, 0.83 HR/9. Jesus Luzardo's 4.85 ERA is misleading — his 28.0% K rate and 6.4% walk rate point to a much better underlying pitcher (his xFIP would be in the mid-3s). When two arms who miss bats and limit walks pitch at a park that kills offense, totals trend under.\n\nBoth offenses are mediocre — SD at 4.06 R/G, PHI at 4.02 R/G. Neither lineup is going to bludgeon its way past pitching of this quality at this park. -115 implies 53.5% — we make the under closer to 55%. Edge is thin but the data lines up.
picks.the_market: Total 7.5 since open, under prices vary from -115 (DK) to -125 (BetMGM). DK is the clear best for the under.
picks.weather_park: Petco Park, mild SoCal weather, marine layer often suppresses fly balls — net under-leaning.
picks.case_against: Luzardo's ERA says he's giving up runs in bunches. If he runs into a 3-run inning, the under math gets ugly fast. Padres bullpen has been shakier than Vasquez and a late inning could blow the total. Both offenses have power bats (Tatis, Schwarber, Harper) who can change a total with one swing.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6 IP at 2-3 ER each, bullpens not coughing up a late frame, final score 4-3 or lower.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Two arms who miss bats, one of the league's quietest pitcher's parks, two so-so offenses. The math says under.
picks.slate_assessment: Loaded 15-game MLB board, one NBA playoff game (Knicks-Cavs), one NHL game (Hurricanes-Habs) — multiple soft starters create clear ladder spots and a clean prop edge against Lodolo at Citi.
picks.executive_summary: Big 15-game MLB board with a Knicks-Cavs playoff tilt and a Hurricanes-Canadiens NHL game on the side. The standout angle is Cincinnati's Nick Lodolo limping in with a 7.20 ERA and 13.4% walk rate against Nolan McLean at pitcher-friendly Citi Field — Mets ML headlines the day. Misiorowski (1.89 ERA, 39.3% K%) at home over Liberatore is the highest-floor straight play, and Yesavage (1.07) against Janson Junk pads the card. Ladder rides Cubs ML — Ben Brown's 2.09 ERA against Mlodzinski is the cleanest near-even-money spot.
picks.slate_analysis:
Fifteen MLB games anchor the board, with a Game 2-style Knicks-Cavs tilt and a Hurricanes-Canadiens NHL game on the side. Pricing climate is normal — most MLB sides sit between -130 and +130, which gives us room to ladder without parlaying. A few starters jump off the page as exploitable: Lodolo (7.20 ERA, 2.40 HR/9, 13.4% BB%), Liberatore (4.70 ERA, 1.58 HR/9 against an elite Misiorowski), Tatsuya Imai (8.31 ERA, 16.9% BB% — a walking disaster), and Janson Junk (5.07) opposite a 1.07 Yesavage. That's four soft-starter spots to target.

Top candidates that rose: (1) Mets ML at -160 vs Reds — McLean's 3.57 ERA, 29.5% K rate, pitcher's park vs Lodolo's free-pass parade. (2) Brewers -1.5 / Brewers ML — Misiorowski's 1.89 ERA and 39.3% K% is the best pitcher edge on the board, but ML at -220 violates our -150 juice cap. (3) Cubs ML at -122 — Ben Brown (2.09 ERA, 0.98 WHIP) is materially better than Mlodzinski, and the price is near even money. (4) Blue Jays -1.5 at +135 — Yesavage 1.07 ERA, 0 HR allowed, vs Junk's 5.07 ERA. (5) Rangers ML vs Imai — Imai's 8.31 ERA and 16.9% walk rate at neutral Globe Life is a fade spot. (6) Lodolo Ks Under 4.5 if listed — not in props pack, so passed. (7) Padres-Phillies under 7.5 — both arms in a pitcher's park.

What got cut: Brewers ML (-220 exceeds juice cap); Dodgers ML (-320, same reason); Astros ML at +113 considered but Imai's volatility is too high to back the side — went Rangers instead; Padres under 7.5 at -115 was close but Luzardo's K upside (28% K) caps the over while Vasquez has been steady — leaned away because the under price is juiced and Phillies offense plays up; Knicks-Cavs Game 4 — no NET/DRTG data in pack and I won't blind-bet a playoff total without confirmed availability; Hurricanes ML at -137 — limited NHL data in pack, no goalie info, pass without confirmation; Cubs/Pirates total stays off — Ben Brown's K-rate suppresses while Mlodzinski is decent, market priced it correctly at 8.

Final six: Mets ML (best edge on the board, 2.0u), Brewers RL -1.5 (Misiorowski dominance, 1.5u), Cubs ML (LADDER — cleanest near-even pitcher edge), Blue Jays -1.5 (Yesavage form), Rangers ML (fade Imai), and Lodolo opposition via Brandon Nimmo TB Over 1.5 — wait, Nimmo's not in this game. Switching the sixth pick to Padres/Phillies Under 7.5 at -115 — Petco PF 0.93, both starters keep the ball in the yard. Ladder is Cubs ML: lowest-variance, near-even-money, best pitcher mismatch at that price.

picks.headline: Tatsuya Imai's 16.9% walk rate is the worst on the board tonight.
picks.the_thesis: Tatsuya Imai is a mess: 8.31 ERA, 1.79 WHIP, 16.9% walk rate, 2.11 HR/9 across 17.1 IP. The K rate (25.3%) is encouraging in isolation but he can't find the strike zone — when you walk one in six batters, K's don't save you. He's averaging 5.7 IP per start, which means he's not even giving the Astros length.\n\nKumar Rocker is no ace (3.60 ERA, 19.1% K%) but he's a competent league-average arm at home in a neutral-to-slight-hitter's park. The gap between the two starters is the entire edge. At -126 FanDuel, implied is 55.7% — we make this closer to 61%. Houston's offense is solid but limited by their starter — Imai will either walk himself out of the game in 4 IP or hit-batsman his way into a 5+ ER inning. Rangers ML is the play.

the_data.label: Imai ERA / BB% / HR/9
the_data.context: Worst SP profile on the slate

picks.the_market: ML -126 to -136 across books, FanDuel best. Total 8.0-8.5 with mixed pricing — sharp money has the side, not the total.
picks.weather_park: Globe Life Field indoor — neutral park, no weather.
picks.case_against: Imai's high K rate means he can still post a 5-IP, 3-ER, 8-K outing on a day his command is workable. Texas offense is the weakest part of this thesis — 3.87 R/G means even with free baserunners, they can leave them on. Houston scoring 5+ off Rocker is in play.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Imai walking himself out by the 5th, Texas cashing in 4+ runs, Rocker grinding through 5-6.
picks.scott_bot_quip: When the line on a starter's walk rate reads like a bowling score, we don't need to overthink it.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis was directionally correct — Imai walked three of the first four batters he faced and loaded the bases in the first inning, exactly the meltdown scenario we modeled. But he escaped via a double play, then retired 16 of the final 17 Rangers he faced en route to six no-hit innings on 97 pitches. Relievers Steven Okert and debutant Alimber Santa finished the combined no-hitter — the first in MLB since 2024 — in a 9-0 Houston win. Rocker, the 'competent league-average arm' we backed, allowed four runs in five innings and took the loss. The pick lost not because the Imai walk-rate thesis was wrong (he walked four on the night), but because a historically rare event — a combined no-hitter thrown by a pitcher entering with an 8.31 ERA — intervened. Texas was no-hit while only striking out three times; they simply couldn't find a hit despite making contact all night.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, pure VARIANCE — a combined no-hitter is a ~0.3% per-game event; no rule can be derived from this outcome.

picks.headline: Yesavage 1.07 ERA, zero homers allowed; Janson Junk in the other dugout.
picks.the_thesis: Trey Yesavage's debut stretch reads like a video game: 1.07 ERA, 28.7% K rate, 0 home runs allowed across 25.1 IP. Tiny sample, sure, but every underlying stat is elite — he's missing bats and not yielding hard contact. Janson Junk is a back-end starter with a 5.07 ERA, a 17.2% K rate (well below average), and a 1.31 HR/9 against a Toronto lineup that punishes mistakes.\n\nML at -160 isn't enough juice headroom for our taste, but the runline at +135 is right in the sweet spot. With Yesavage likely keeping Miami under 2 runs and the Jays getting at Junk for 4+, a 2-run margin is more often than +135 implies (~42.5%). We make it ~49% — clean edge.
picks.the_market: Total 7.5, ML -160 across books, RL +135 at BetMGM is best of three. Numbers stable since open.
picks.weather_park: Rogers Centre, indoor — neutral park, no weather impact.
picks.case_against: Yesavage's 25 IP sample is thin enough that he could throw a clunker tonight. Junk has stretches where he throws strikes and Miami's offense (4.28 R/G) is competent enough to keep a one-run game. RL is always vulnerable to bullpen meltdowns late.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Yesavage 6+ IP at 1 ER, Toronto putting up 5+ on Junk and the Marlins bullpen, final margin 2+.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Yesavage hasn't allowed a homer in 25 innings. Janson Junk has allowed eight in 55. We know what we're doing here.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis rested on Yesavage being Yesavage — but this was the start where the small sample finally regressed. He allowed a career-high five runs on five hits across 6.2 innings, surrendering more earned runs in one outing than he had in his previous five starts combined. Meanwhile, Junk — the supposed piñata — held Toronto to one run on eight singles across five innings, walking nobody and winning for the first time since April 28. The model correctly identified the pitching gap but built a run-line bet on a 25-inning ERA that was always one bad start away from evaporating; the 'case against' section even flagged this exact scenario and was overruled. Compounding matters, Vladimir Guerrero Jr. sat out with an elbow injury, quietly removing Toronto's most dangerous bat from the lineup the model priced in.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Do not back a run-line favorite when the anchor starter has fewer than 40 IP in the current season AND the implied win margin requires both the starter to hold AND the opposing back-end arm to implode — two independent events that must co-occur; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the failure mode (tiny-sample ERA regression + key lineup scratch) is a known structural risk that the pick's own 'case against' section identified and dismissed.

picks.headline: Ben Brown's 2.09 ERA against Mlodzinski's 3.96 — best pitcher edge near even money.
picks.the_thesis: Ben Brown has been quietly excellent: 2.09 ERA, 0.98 WHIP, 26.0% K rate, 7.8% walk rate. That's a borderline ace line over 38.2 IP. Mlodzinski is a serviceable mid-rotation arm (3.96 ERA, 20.4% K%, 1.40 WHIP) but the WHIP gap is the giveaway — Brown is suppressing baserunners at nearly half the rate. PNC Park is pitcher-friendly (0.95 runs, 0.91 HR), which actually helps both arms but mutes Pittsburgh's already-modest offense more than it hurts a strikeout pitcher like Brown.\n\nThe Cubs offense (Busch, Crow-Armstrong, Happ, Swanson) is materially deeper than Pittsburgh's, and at -122 the implied price is 55% — we make this closer to 60%. Clean edge.
picks.the_market: ML opened -122 implied 55%, sits at -122 to -125 across 3 books. Stable, sharp money hasn't moved it but the price still has value at DK.
picks.weather_park: PNC Park, 74°F, light cross-breeze, HR factor 0.91 — net neutral, favors pitching.
picks.case_against: Brown's 38 IP is a thin sample for the 2.09 ERA — his peripherals (K%, BB%) say he's good, but not 2.09 good. If he regresses to his FIP, the gap closes. Pittsburgh at home with a solid Mlodzinski outing and the Cubs slipping into a quiet offensive night is the bust path.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Brown going 6 strong, Cubs scratching 3-4 runs off Mlodzinski and the soft Pirates pen.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Best price, best floor, near even money. The boys' ladder doesn't need fireworks.
picks.ladder_note: Highest floor on the board at a near-even-money price. Brown is the most dominant SP we can back without paying -200+ juice, and Pittsburgh's bottom-third offense limits the variance. Clean spot to double up.
picks.slate_assessment: Loaded 15-game MLB board, one NBA playoff game (Knicks-Cavs), one NHL game (Hurricanes-Habs) — multiple soft starters create clear ladder spots and a clean prop edge against Lodolo at Citi.
picks.executive_summary: Big 15-game MLB board with a Knicks-Cavs playoff tilt and a Hurricanes-Canadiens NHL game on the side. The standout angle is Cincinnati's Nick Lodolo limping in with a 7.20 ERA and 13.4% walk rate against Nolan McLean at pitcher-friendly Citi Field — Mets ML headlines the day. Misiorowski (1.89 ERA, 39.3% K%) at home over Liberatore is the highest-floor straight play, and Yesavage (1.07) against Janson Junk pads the card. Ladder rides Cubs ML — Ben Brown's 2.09 ERA against Mlodzinski is the cleanest near-even-money spot.
picks.slate_analysis:
Fifteen MLB games anchor the board, with a Game 2-style Knicks-Cavs tilt and a Hurricanes-Canadiens NHL game on the side. Pricing climate is normal — most MLB sides sit between -130 and +130, which gives us room to ladder without parlaying. A few starters jump off the page as exploitable: Lodolo (7.20 ERA, 2.40 HR/9, 13.4% BB%), Liberatore (4.70 ERA, 1.58 HR/9 against an elite Misiorowski), Tatsuya Imai (8.31 ERA, 16.9% BB% — a walking disaster), and Janson Junk (5.07) opposite a 1.07 Yesavage. That's four soft-starter spots to target.

Top candidates that rose: (1) Mets ML at -160 vs Reds — McLean's 3.57 ERA, 29.5% K rate, pitcher's park vs Lodolo's free-pass parade. (2) Brewers -1.5 / Brewers ML — Misiorowski's 1.89 ERA and 39.3% K% is the best pitcher edge on the board, but ML at -220 violates our -150 juice cap. (3) Cubs ML at -122 — Ben Brown (2.09 ERA, 0.98 WHIP) is materially better than Mlodzinski, and the price is near even money. (4) Blue Jays -1.5 at +135 — Yesavage 1.07 ERA, 0 HR allowed, vs Junk's 5.07 ERA. (5) Rangers ML vs Imai — Imai's 8.31 ERA and 16.9% walk rate at neutral Globe Life is a fade spot. (6) Lodolo Ks Under 4.5 if listed — not in props pack, so passed. (7) Padres-Phillies under 7.5 — both arms in a pitcher's park.

What got cut: Brewers ML (-220 exceeds juice cap); Dodgers ML (-320, same reason); Astros ML at +113 considered but Imai's volatility is too high to back the side — went Rangers instead; Padres under 7.5 at -115 was close but Luzardo's K upside (28% K) caps the over while Vasquez has been steady — leaned away because the under price is juiced and Phillies offense plays up; Knicks-Cavs Game 4 — no NET/DRTG data in pack and I won't blind-bet a playoff total without confirmed availability; Hurricanes ML at -137 — limited NHL data in pack, no goalie info, pass without confirmation; Cubs/Pirates total stays off — Ben Brown's K-rate suppresses while Mlodzinski is decent, market priced it correctly at 8.

Final six: Mets ML (best edge on the board, 2.0u), Brewers RL -1.5 (Misiorowski dominance, 1.5u), Cubs ML (LADDER — cleanest near-even pitcher edge), Blue Jays -1.5 (Yesavage form), Rangers ML (fade Imai), and Lodolo opposition via Brandon Nimmo TB Over 1.5 — wait, Nimmo's not in this game. Switching the sixth pick to Padres/Phillies Under 7.5 at -115 — Petco PF 0.93, both starters keep the ball in the yard. Ladder is Cubs ML: lowest-variance, near-even-money, best pitcher mismatch at that price.

autopsy.post_mortem: Ben Brown delivered exactly the gem the thesis demanded — he was dominant through his outing, with Brandon Lowe's RBI double in the 3rd being the only real damage Brown allowed. The Cubs tied it 1-1 on a Michael Busch solo HR in the 5th, and the game was still very much alive. The loss turned on a single at-bat in the 7th: reliever Trent Thornton surrendered a solo home run to light-hitting catcher Henry Davis, a low-leverage, low-probability outcome that handed Pittsburgh a 2-1 lead the Cubs never recovered from. The Cubs offense went 6-for-the-game with just one extra-base hit and stranded five runners in the first three innings alone — but the decisive run was a bullpen blowup against a bottom-of-the-order bat, not a starter failure or a lineup collapse.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The thesis (starter edge + lineup depth) was sound and nearly correct — this is a one-game bullpen variance event, not a signal.

picks.headline: Misiorowski's 39.3% K rate vs Liberatore's 1.58 HR/9 — and we get plus money on the runline.
picks.the_thesis: Jacob Misiorowski is having a Cy Young-caliber season — 1.89 ERA, 0.88 WHIP, and a 39.3% strikeout rate. To frame that K rate: anything above 30% is elite, 39% is borderline historic. He goes 6+ innings a start and his Brewers bullpen has been one of the league's best behind him.\n\nMatthew Liberatore comes in at a 4.70 ERA, 1.55 WHIP, and a 1.58 HR/9 — that homer rate is brutal and exactly what gets exposed against Milwaukee's 4.92 R/G offense at home. The Brewers ML at -220 is too juiced for our straight-bet cap, so the runline at even money is the way to capitalize. With Misiorowski likely keeping STL to 1-2 runs and MIL likely scoring 4+ on Liberatore, a 2+ run margin is in play more often than the +100 price suggests.
picks.the_market: Total 7.5, MIL -1.5 at +100 on FanDuel — DK and MGM are -107 and -110, so FanDuel is the clear best price. ML at -220 violates juice cap, runline becomes the play.
picks.weather_park: Indoor at American Family Field, neutral park — no weather variable.
picks.case_against: Runlines die on one-run wins. If Misiorowski exits at 95 pitches in the 6th with a 3-1 lead and the Brewers pen gives up a late run, the under-the-spread outcome is annoying but real. Liberatore can grind out 5 IP of 3-run ball even on bad days.
picks.what_were_betting_on: Misiorowski 6+ IP, 1-2 ER; Milwaukee crossing 4+ on Liberatore; final margin 2+ runs.
picks.scott_bot_quip: Even money on the better team, the better pitcher, and the better bullpen. We'll take that math.
picks.slate_assessment: Loaded 15-game MLB board, one NBA playoff game (Knicks-Cavs), one NHL game (Hurricanes-Habs) — multiple soft starters create clear ladder spots and a clean prop edge against Lodolo at Citi.

Ladder rides Cubs ML — Ben Brown's 2.09 ERA against Mlodzinski is the cleanest near-even-money spot.
